Question: Two owners have separate deeds but equal ownership. What form of ownership is this?
Feedback / Explanation: Tenants in common allows each owner to hold a separate deed while sharing ownership.

Question: Red River, a navigable stream, forms the boundary between farmer Brown's property and that of a neighbor, Smith. Neighbor Smith allows a canoeist to cross the property to gain access to the river. The water traffic annoys farmer Brown. Does Brown have the right to stop the canoeist from using the river?
A: No, since Red River is a navigable stream.
B: Yes, since Brown is a riparian owner and owns to the center of the stream.
C: Yes, since Brown is a riparian owner and owns the bed of the stream.
D: Since Brown is a riparian owner, he owns the stream to the middle and can prevent the canoeist from using his one-half of the stream.
Feedback / Explanation: Riparian owners on navigable streams own to the center line and may restrict access to their half.

Question: Which of the following cannot be depreciated for tax purposes?
Feedback / Explanation: Land is not subject to wear and tear, so it cannot be depreciated.

Question: What is the best way to determine if there is an encroachment on your property?
Feedback / Explanation: A survey precisely shows boundaries and reveals encroachments.

Question: A zoning law states that no structure on any lot can be constructed within 15 feet of the lot line. One owner obtained a one-time approval from the zoning board to build to a point 10 feet from the lot line. What did the zoning board grant that owner?
Feedback / Explanation: A variance allows deviation from zoning requirements for one property.

Question: In real estate sales, when a cooperating sub-agent performs acts, the agent is:
A: Not responsible since they are with another company.
B: Responsible only if they know of the acts.
C: Fully responsible for the acts of the sub-agent.
D: Partially responsible for the acts of the sub-agent.
Feedback / Explanation: An agent is liable if they are aware of the sub-agents improper acts.

Question: Destruction of subject property terminates the agency relationship by:
A: Consent of the agent and principal
Feedback / Explanation: Loss of the property ends the agency automatically by law.

Question: Property owned by James was sold at a tax sale. He may regain full ownership by payment of the taxes, interest, and other charges within a specified time. What is this process called?
Feedback / Explanation: The statutory right of redemption lets an owner reclaim property after a tax sale.

Question: What is usually true of contracts made by minors?
A: They are enforceable by all parties.
C: They are voidable by the vendee.
D: They are voidable by the vendor.
Feedback / Explanation: Minors can enforce contracts, but others cannot enforce against them without their consent.

Question: An easement passes over an Smiths land to allow Jones access to an adjacent parcel owned by Jones. What term would apply to Jones land?
Feedback / Explanation: The dominant estate benefits from the easement.

Question: Which of the following would be considered a violation of the Fair Housing Act of 1968?
A: A private club owns a building for the use of its members only and refuses to rent a unit to a couple who are not members.
B: A man who only owns one home refuses to sell to a man of Asian descent.
C: A man who owns four houses refuses to rent to a woman with two children.
D: A lender refuses to make a loan to Mr. Jack, an African American male, because Mr. Jack has been unemployed for several months and has a history of unemployment.
Feedback / Explanation: Refusing to rent to a family with children is discrimination based on familial status.

Question: If the principal instructs the broker-in-charge to violate the law, what should the broker-in-charge do?
A: Refuse to obey and give the principal the name of another brokerage that would probably do as instructed.
D: Proceed very carefully to avoid prosecution.
Feedback / Explanation: The broker must withdraw to avoid participating in illegal acts.

Question: What law allows borrowers who have been denied credit to view the credit report that was used to make the determination?
Feedback / Explanation: The Fair Credit Reporting Act gives the right to review credit reports used in decisions.

Question: Why may a license be suspended or revoked?
A: For making unkind remarks about your competition.
B: For working for the Buyer and the Seller.
C: For fraudulent misrepresentation in a real estate transaction.
D: For refusing to join the Board of Realtors.
Feedback / Explanation: Fraud in a real estate transaction is grounds for loss of license.

Question: If a group of competing real estate brokers-in-charge created a "fee schedule" of commission rates that all the brokerages agreed to follow, what law would they be violating?
Feedback / Explanation: Price-fixing among competitors violates the Sherman Antitrust Act.

Question: Which action may the South Carolina Real Estate Commission take?
A: Suspend, revoke or cancel any license it issues.
B: Imprison a violator of the license laws.
C: Both fine and imprison violators of license laws.
D: Fine a licensee a maximum of $1000.
Feedback / Explanation: The Commission can discipline by suspending, revoking, or canceling licenses.

Question: Wha may cause a licensee to lose their license?
A: Buying listed properties as an undisclosed principal.
B: Failing to be employed on a full-time basis.
Feedback / Explanation: Failing to disclose when buying property for oneself is prohibited.

Question: The Fair Housing Act has as its primary purpose the:
B: Creation of equal but separate housing.
C: Creating of minority employment in the United States.
D: Creation and assurance of fair housing opportunities for everyone.
Feedback / Explanation: It ensures equal housing opportunities regardless of protected classes.

Question: When applying for a VA loan the veteran must have a document from the Veteran's Administration stating that the veteran is eligible for the loan. This is called a/an:
Feedback / Explanation: The Certificate of Eligibility proves the veteran qualifies for a VA loan.

Question: Under the law of contracts when something remains to be done, the contract is said to be a/an ____________contract. When all the conditions of the contract have been met, the contract is said to be a/an ____________contract.
Feedback / Explanation: An executory contract is incomplete; executed means fully performed.

Question: In what type of contract do both parties promise to perform specified acts?
Feedback / Explanation: A bilateral contract contains mutual promises from both sides.

Question: Broker Betty wants to show a property. Betty finds out that a competing brokerage has an exclusive right to sell listing on the property. How should Betty proceed?
B: Ask permission from listing agent to show the property.
C: Hold off until the listing expires.
D: Advise her customer that state law requires her to be a "Buyer's Agent."
Feedback / Explanation: Permission from the listing agent is required to show the property.

Question: Which of the following statements is true concerning a "Quitclaim Deed"?
A: It carries no warranties or covenants.
B: It warrants that there are no covenants except those recorded.
C: It warrants against encumbrances, if any, which are known to the grantor.
D: It warrants against encumbrances known by the grantee.
Feedback / Explanation: Quitclaim deeds transfer any interest without warranties.

Question: If the owner of a property solicits a buyer and sells the property to that buyer while you have an exclusive agency listing, how much commission do you receive?
B: The commission shown in the listing agreement that you would get if you sold the property.
C: The split stated in the cooperating agency agreement.
D: On half of the stated commission.
Feedback / Explanation: Exclusive agency allows the owner to sell directly without paying commission.

Question: What is the primary purpose of the South Carolina Real Estate Commission?
C: Protect citizens in the real estate business.
D: Protect the public through police power.
Feedback / Explanation: The Commissions purpose is to protect the public interest.

Question: George pays Dan a sum of money which gives George the right to buy a specific parcel from Dan, within a specified period, and for a specific price. Dan remains in possession of the property. What has George acquired?
Feedback / Explanation: An option is the right to buy at set terms within a certain time.

Question: A right, benefit, or improvement, that belongs to and becomes part of the real estate is best described as a/an:
Feedback / Explanation: An appurtenance is a right or improvement attached to and passing with the property.

Question: A lawsuit in which am African American man was denied the right to purchase a home because of his race became a landmark civil rights decision because it reaffirmed the 1866 Civil Rights Act. What is the name of this case?
Feedback / Explanation: Jones v. Mayer confirmed no racial discrimination in property sales.

Question: What is the experience requirement to become a broker in South Carolina?
A: Six months with an active Associate license
B: One year with an active Associate license
C: Five of the past seven years with an Associate license
D: Three of the past five years with an Associate license
Feedback / Explanation: SC requires 5 years as a licensed salesperson in the last 7 years.

Question: What is an unlicensed administrative employee of a broker-in-charge allowed to do?
A: Sell property if it is done under the broker-in-charges direct supervision.
B: Sell or negotiate deals if they are done in the office.
C: Refer interested customers to the broker-in-charge or other licensed individuals in the office.
D: Show property if price is not discussed.
Feedback / Explanation: Unlicensed staff can refer prospects to a licensee but not sell or negotiate.

Question: Once they have completed the 60-hour Unit I Pre-License course, how long do students have to wait before they can take the 30-hour Unit II Advanced Principles course?
B: Until they pass the State licensing exam
Feedback / Explanation: Both courses can be taken back-to-back with no wait required.

Question: Where does the South Carolina Real Estate Commission receive its police power to protect the public?
C: The Department of Labor Licensing and Regulation
Feedback / Explanation: The state legislature grants the Commissions authority.

Question: Who would not be required to have a real estate license?
B: Party selling neighbors home for a fee
D: Attorney selling a client's property in connection with other legal duties
Feedback / Explanation: Attorneys acting within their duties are exempt from licensing.

Question: Which is not a duty of the South Carolina Real Estate Commission?
B: Promulgating regulations governing the real estate business.
Feedback / Explanation: The Commission does not arbitrate disputes between licensees.

Question: Roberts leaves town and a neighbor, Smiley, sells Roberts' home with Roberts' permission and charges no fee. Which choice is true?
B: Smiley is guilty of a misdemeanor.
C: Smiley should have charged a reasonable fee
D: Roberts can be fined for his actions in this matter.
Feedback / Explanation: Selling without compensation and without license does not violate license law.

Question: A party living in an open, notorious, and hostile manner on a parcel of land for the time prescribed by law may, under some circumstances, gain title through what process?
Feedback / Explanation: Adverse possession allows legal title after statutory occupation period.

Question: When an supervised licensee leaves a brokerage, the broker-in-charge should take what action?
A: Allow the license to expire and not renew since they don't work there anymore.
B: Give the license to the supervised licensee.
D: Return the license to the Real Estate Commission.
Feedback / Explanation: The broker must return the license to the SC Real Estate Commission.

Question: What is the intended benefit of the truth-in-lending portion of the Truth-in-Lending Integrated Disclosure (TRID) legislation?
B: It protects borrowers by allowing them to compare the cost of borrowing from various firms.
C: It protects borrowers from paying exorbitant interest rates.
D: It protects both borrowers and lenders from frivolous lawsuits.
Feedback / Explanation: It allows borrowers to compare loan terms from different lenders.

Question: How do appraisers normally obtain the square footage of single-family detached homes?
Feedback / Explanation: Exterior measurements are standard for determining square footage.

Question: A building supply company that delivered shingles to a construction site and did not get paid by the builder may file a ______________.
Feedback / Explanation: A mechanics lien protects suppliers for unpaid materials or labor.

Question: Bo owns a large tract of land, and his home is next to the road. Bo sells the back part of his lot to John. At the time of the sale Bo gave John the right to use a 20-foot strip of land across his property for a driveway. This is an _______________.
Feedback / Explanation: Easement appurtenant benefits the back lot and runs with the land.

Question: What do we call any claim, charge, or liability which attaches to and is binding on the real estate is?
Feedback / Explanation: An encumbrance is any burden or limitation on property rights.

Question: Through a condemnation suit the government may acquire private property and convert it to public use under the power of ________________.
Feedback / Explanation: Eminent domain is the power to take property with compensation.

Question: A permissive use that gives no rights to the user because it can be revoked at any time is known as a/an _______________.
Feedback / Explanation: A license is temporary and can be revoked at will.

Question: All the following are private control of property except which one?
Feedback / Explanation: Zoning ordinances are public (government) controls.

Question: A property use that was legal in the beginning but is now in violation of the current zoning regulations would be a:
Feedback / Explanation: Non-conforming use is grandfathered from before zoning changed.

Question: The document that shows a short history of the title, including the chain of title, would be the ______________.
Feedback / Explanation: An abstract summarizes the chain of title and recorded documents.

Question: All the following are requirements for a valid deed except which one?
Feedback / Explanation: Only the grantor must sign; grantees signature not required.

Question: What type of deed creates the greatest liability for the grantor?
Feedback / Explanation: General warranty deed guarantees title against all defects forever.

Question: What type of deed provides warranty by a prior owner only after they acquired title?
Feedback / Explanation: Special warranty limits guarantees to the grantors period of ownership.

Question: Title insurance would normally cover all the following except for:
D: Changes in land use brought about by zoning
Feedback / Explanation: Zoning changes are not insurable title defects.

Question: What is the covenant in a general warranty deed whereby the grantor promises to compensate the grantee for any loss suffered because of the condition of the title, other than disclosed encumbrances?
Feedback / Explanation: Warranty forever promises to defend title against claims.

Question: When a grantor gives a document to transfer a possible interest in the property, which clears up defects in the title, the grantee might receive a ________________.
Feedback / Explanation: Quitclaim deeds release any interest, clearing potential title issues.

Question: When a person dies intestate and with no heirs found, the state has a reversionary interest called ________________.
Feedback / Explanation: Escheat returns property to the state when no heirs exist.

Question: The procedures used to record real estate title documents are governed by ________________.
D: Rules of various federal regulatory agencies
Feedback / Explanation: Recording is regulated at the state level.

Question: A recorded deed must have all but which of the following?
Feedback / Explanation: A deed need not list encumbrances to be valid for recording.

Question: Cities and counties incorporate their long-term land usage goals into what types of documents?
Feedback / Explanation: A master plan guides future growth and land use.

Question: The legal premise underlying the definition of littoral rights is that a lake or sea
A: is not a navigable body of water
B: should be available to the owner for unrestricted use
C: is a navigable body of water
D: is owned by the abutting owners
Feedback / Explanation: The legal premise underlying the definition of littoral rights is that a lake or sea is a navigable body of water and, therefore, public property owned by the state

Question: Three unique physical characteristics of land are immobility, indestructibility and
Feedback / Explanation: Land has three unique physical characteristics: immobility, indestructibility, and heterogeneity.  It cannot be moved, destroyed or identical to any other property

Question: Land cannot be homogenous because
B: a parcel of land cannot be moved
Feedback / Explanation: Land is non-homogeneous since no two parcels of land are exactly the same. They are inherently different because each parcel has a unique location

Question: Which of the following concepts is associated with the term real property?
Feedback / Explanation: Real property is ownership of real estate and the bundle of rights associated with owning the real estate

Question: Which of the following statements is true regarding the bundle of rights?
A: An owner cannot transfer subsurface rights without transferring air rights
B: An owner can transfer subsurface rights without transferring air rights
C: An owner cannot relinquish the right to own the subsurface rights
D: An owner can never divide the bundle of rights.
Feedback / Explanation: The bundle of real property rights also applies separately to the individual components of real estate: the air, the surface, and the subsurface. An owner can, for example, transfer subsurface rights without transferring air rights

Question: Which of the following is an example of a trade fixture?
Feedback / Explanation: Trade fixtures, or chattel fixtures, are items of a tenant's personal property that the tenant has temporarily affixed to a landlord's real property in order to conduct business. Examples of trade fixtures include a grocer's food freezers, a merchant's clothes racks, a tavern owner's bar, a dairy's milking machines, and a printer's printing press

Question: Plants and crops that grow naturally without requiring anyone's labor or machinery are considered
Feedback / Explanation: Plants and crops that grow naturally without requiring anyone's labor or machinery are considered real property. Plants and crops requiring human intervention and labor are called emblements

Question: The conversion of real property to personal property effected by detaching it from the real estate is known as
Feedback / Explanation: Severance is the conversion of real property to personal property by detaching it from the real estate, such as by cutting down a tree, detaching a door from a shed, or removing an antenna from a roof

Question: Which of the following statements regarding the conversion of property is true?
A: The classification of an item of property as real or personal is not necessarily fixed
B: The classification of property can never be changed by the process of conversion
C: The conversion of real property to personal property is not possible
D: The conversion of personal property to real property is not possible
Feedback / Explanation: The classification of an item of property as real or personal is not necessarily fixed. The classification may be changed by the process of conversion

Question: Real estate includes such things as fences, streets, buildings, wells, sewers, sidewalks, and piers. Such man-made structures attached to the land are called
Feedback / Explanation: Real estate includes, in addition to land, such things as fences, streets, buildings, wells, sewers, sidewalks, and piers. Such man-made structures attached to the land are called improvements

Question: The ownership of real estate and the bundle of rights associated with owning the real estate is defined as
Feedback / Explanation: Real property is ownership of real estate and the bundle of rights associated with owning the real estate

Question: Which of the following is an example of tangible property?
Feedback / Explanation: All real estate, by its physical nature, is tangible property. Personal property may be tangible or intangible. Boats, jewelry, coins, appliances, computers, and art work are examples of tangible personal property. Stocks, copyrights, bonds, trademarks, patents, franchises, and listing agreements are examples of intangible personal property

Question: The right to ____________ the property includes the right to mortgage the property as collateral for debt
Feedback / Explanation: The right to encumber the property includes the right to mortgage the property as collateral for debt. There may be restrictions to this right, such as a spouse's right to limit the degree to which a homestead may be mortgaged

Question: Since water is a resource necessary for survival, some states -- particularly those where water is scarce -- have taken the legal position that the state owns and controls all bodies of water. Which of the following applies to property owners that must obtain permits for the use of water?
Feedback / Explanation: Called the Doctrine of Prior Appropriation, this legality requires that property owners obtain permits for use of water. If a proposed usage is reasonable and beneficial, the state will grant a permit which, over time, can attach to the property of the permit holder

Question: If a body of water is entirely contained within the boundaries of an owner's property,  the owner would have which of the following rights?
Feedback / Explanation: The legal premise underlying the definition of littoral rights is that a lake or sea is a navigable body of water and, therefore, public property owned by the state. By contrast, a body of water entirely contained within the boundaries of an owner's property is not navigable. In such a case, the owner would own the water as well as unrestricted rights of usage

Question: Which of the following statements is true regarding a property owners littoral rights?
A: The ownership extends to the low watermark of the body of water
B: The ownership extends to the high watermark of the body of water
C: The owner enjoys unrestricted use of the water and owns the land beneath
D: The owner owns the land beneath the body of water to the waters midstream
Feedback / Explanation: Owners of properties abutting a navigable, non-moving body of water enjoy the littoral right of use but do not own the water nor the land beneath the water. Ownership extends to the high-water mark of the body of water

Question: The primary criterion for distinguishing real property from personal property is
A: whether the item is permanently attached to the land or the structure
B: the original cost of the item
C: whether the item was included in the previous sale of the property
D: whether the item will be included in the listing agreement
Feedback / Explanation: The primary criterion for distinguishing real from personal property is whether the item is permanently attached to the land or structures attached to the land

Question: The act of converting personal property to real property by attaching it to real estate is known as
Feedback / Explanation: Affixing, or attachment, is the act of converting personal property to real property by attaching it to the real estate, such as by assembling a pile of bricks into a barbecue pit, or constructing a boat dock from wood planks

Question: Which of the following is the primary regulatory entity for the real estate brokerage business?
Feedback / Explanation: State governments are the primary regulatory entities of the real estate brokerage business. State governments establish real estate license laws and qualifications. In addition, state governments have established real estate commissions to administer license laws and oversee activities of licensees

Question: The judicial system exerts an influence on real estate ownership and use through decisions based on
Feedback / Explanation: The judicial system exerts an influence on real estate ownership and use through decisions based on case law and common law, as distinguished from statutory law. Case law consists of decisions based on judicial precedent. Common law is the collective body of law deriving from custom and generally accepted practice in society

Question: "Real estate" includes such things as
A: fences, sidewalks, and piers on a parcel of land
B: mobile homes temporarily parked on a parcel of land
C: timber that has been cut and is lying on a parcel of land
D: business equipment an owner or tenant has placed on a parcel of land
Feedback / Explanation: The legal concept of real estate encompasses the land and all man-made structures that are "permanently" attached to the land. Real estate therefore includes, in addition to land, such things as fences, streets, buildings, wells, sewers, sidewalks and piers. The phrase "permanently attached" refers primarily to one's intention in attaching the item

Question: An "improvement" is
A: a modification of land to make it more useful
B: a modification of a structure to enhance its value
C: a structure intended as a permanent attachment to land
D: a parcel of land that has been prepared for development
Feedback / Explanation: Real estate therefore includes, in addition to land, such things as fences, streets, buildings, wells, sewers, sidewalks and piers. Such man-made structures attached to the land are called improvements

Question: The physical limits of land are
A: the surface of the earth and infinite space above the surface
B: the center of the earth and infinite space above the earth
C: the surface of the earth and all water and minerals on or below the surface to the center of the earth
D: the surface of the earth and the air rights above the surface to the point defined by local zoning
Feedback / Explanation: Subsurface rights apply to land beneath the surface of the real estate parcel extending from its surface boundaries downward to the center of the earth

Question: The "bundle of rights" refers to a set of rights
A: enjoyed by the owner of a property
B: that is inseparable from a parcel of real estate
C: guaranteed to citizens by the Declaration of Independence
D: specified in a deed or land contract
Feedback / Explanation: This group of rights includes the right to Possess, Use, Transfer, Encumber, and Exclude others from using the property. (Remember: "PUTEE") Transfer rights include the right to sell, rent, donate, assign, or bequeath. The owner may also encumber the item by mortgaging it as collateral for debt

Question: The rights to possess and use a property are
C: limited by what the law allows
D: dependent on the type of property involved
Feedback / Explanation: In owning property, one has the right to possess and use it as the law allows

Question: By definition, personal property
B: is any property that is not real property
C: is any property owned by an individual
D: is real property that is not attached to real estate
Feedback / Explanation: Personal property is ownership of anything which is not real estate

Question: The right to encumber a property means that the owner can
B: use the property as collateral for debt
D: assign the bundle of rights to another
Feedback / Explanation: The right to encumber the property essentially means the right to mortgage the property as collateral for debt. There may be restrictions to this right, such as a spouse's right to limit the degree to which a homestead may be mortgaged

Question: A property owner has the right to transfer water rights
A: if the owner has not previously separated the bundle of rights
B: only to the owner of an adjoining property
C: only if there are water rights attached to the property
Feedback / Explanation: While all rights are transferrable, the owner can only transfer what the owner in fact possesses. A property seller, for example, cannot sell water rights if there are no water rights attached to the property

Question: A lease is an example of a(n)
A: transfer of a portion of the bundle of rights
B: encroachment on the bundle of rights
C: transfer of the complete bundle of rights
Feedback / Explanation: An ordinary lease is a common example of the transfer of a portion of one's bundle of rights

Question: Which of the following is included in surface rights?
Feedback / Explanation: Surface rights apply to the real estate contained within the surface boundaries of the parcel. This includes the ground, all natural things affixed to the ground, and all improvements. Surface rights also include water rights

Question: The right to build a structure on top of an existing structure is an example of
Feedback / Explanation: Air rights apply to the space above the surface boundaries of the parcel, as delineated by imaginary vertical lines extended to infinity

Question: Water rights include the right to
A: extract water from the water table
B: divert surface water onto an adjoining property
C: prevent a stream or river from flowing onto an adjoining property
D: own and use a body of water on the surface of the earth
Feedback / Explanation: Water rights basically concern the rights to own and use water found in lakes, streams, rivers, and the ocean

Question: Littoral rights concern which of the following bodies of water?
A: Lakes entirely contained within the boundaries of an owner's property
Feedback / Explanation: Littoral rights concern properties abutting bodies of water that are not moving, such as lakes and seas. Owners of properties abutting a navigable, non-moving body of water enjoy the littoral right of use, but do not own the water nor the land beneath the water. Ownership extends to the high-water mark of the body of water

Question: If littoral rights attach to a property, the property extends to
A: the high water mark of the body of water
B: the low water mark of the body of water
C: the center line of the body of water
D: the property line of a property that also has littoral rights on the opposite side of the body of water
Feedback / Explanation: Ownership extends to the high-water mark of the body of water

Question: Which of the following is true of littoral rights?
A: They revert to the state when the property is sold
C: They are a personal right belonging to an individual owner, not attaching to the real property
D: They transfer with the property when the property is sold
Feedback / Explanation: Littoral rights attach to the property. When the property is sold, the littoral rights transfer with the property to the new owner

Question: Riparian rights concern which of the following bodies of water?
Feedback / Explanation: Riparian rights concern properties abutting moving water such as streams and rivers. If a property abuts a stream or river, the owner's riparian rights are determined by whether the water is navigable or not navigable. If the property abuts a non-navigable stream, the owner enjoys unrestricted use of the water and owns the land beneath the stream to the stream's midpoint. If the waterway in question is navigable, the waterway is considered to be a public easement. In such a case, the owner's property extends to the water's edge as opposed to the midpoint of the waterway

Question: Who owns the land under a navigable river?
A: Owners of properties that abut the river
D: The municipality, if the area is incorporated
Feedback / Explanation: If the waterway in question is navigable, the waterway is considered to be a public easement. In such a case, the owner's property extends to the water's edge as opposed to the midpoint of the waterway. The state owns the land beneath the water

Question: If a property has riparian rights, the owner has the right to
A: pollute the water for a legitimate business purpose
C: require downstream property owners to pay a fee for use of the water
D: use the water in a way that does not infringe on another's riparian rights
Feedback / Explanation: One's riparian rights to use flowing water are subject to the conditions that: the usage is reasonable and does not infringe on the riparian rights of other owners downstream  the usage does not pollute the water  the usage does not impede or alter the course of the water flow

Question: If a property abuts a navigable river, where is the boundary of the property?
C: At the midpoint of the river
Feedback / Explanation: If the waterway in question is navigable, the waterway is considered to be a public easement. In such a case, the owner's property extends to the water's edge

Question: What is a "fixture?"
A: Any item of personal property located within the boundaries of a parcel of real estate
B: An item of personal property that has been converted to real property
C: An item of real property temporarily placed on land for the purpose of conducting a business
D: An item of personal property that has been left in one location for a period of one year
Feedback / Explanation: A personal property item that has been converted to real property by attachment to real estate is called a fixture. Typical examples are chandeliers, toilets, water pumps, septic tanks, and window shutters

Question: Even though it is attached to a structure, an item may be considered personal property rather than real property if
A: the owner originally intended to remove it after a period of time
B: it can be removed without altering the appearance of the structure
C: it is unnecessary to the physical integrity of the structure
D: the owner installed it at some time after acquiring the real property
Feedback / Explanation: One's original intention can override the test of movability in determining whether an item is a fixture or not. If someone attached an item to real property, yet intended to remove it after a period of time, the article may be deemed personal property

Question: The level of government that is the primary regulator of the real estate business is
Feedback / Explanation: State governments are the primary regulatory entities of the real estate business

Question: What guarantees the right of private ownership of real estate in the United States?
Feedback / Explanation: The Constitution establishes absolute right of private ownership of real estate

Question: Which of the following is the best definition of real estate?
C: Land and everything permanently attached to it
D: An ownership interest in land and improvements
Feedback / Explanation: Real estate is the land and all things, man made or natural, that are permanently attached to it

Question: Which of the following is included in the legal concept of land?
A: The surface of the earth and all natural things permanently attached to the earth
B: Only the surface of the earth that is delineated by boundaries
C: The surface of the earth except for lakes and streams
D: Everything above, on and below the surface of the earth
Feedback / Explanation: Land includes minerals beneath the earth's surface, water on or below the earth's surface, and the air above the surface. In addition, land includes all plants attached to the ground or in the ground, such as trees and grass

Question: What are the three unique physical characteristics of land?
Feedback / Explanation: Land has three unique physical characteristics: immobility, indestructibility, and heterogeneity

Question: The primary distinction between the legal concepts of land and real estate is that
A: real estate includes air above the surface and minerals below the surface
D: land does not include man-made structures
Feedback / Explanation: Real estate includes, in addition to land, such things as fences, streets, buildings, wells, sewers, sidewalks and piers. Such manmade structures attached to the land are called improvements

Question: The primary distinction between the legal concepts of real estate and real property is that
A: real property includes ownership of a bundle of rights
C: real property is physical, not abstract
Feedback / Explanation: Real property is ownership of real estate and the bundle of rights associated with owning the real estate

Question: Which of the following is included in the bundle of rights inherent in ownership?
Feedback / Explanation: In owning property, the owner has the right to transfer ownership of the item (sell, rent, donate, assign, or bequeath)

Question: Which of the following is an example of intangible property?
Feedback / Explanation: A stock certificate, contract, or patent would be an example of intangible property

Question: The right to use real property is limited by
A: the right of others to use and enjoy their property
D: Title 12 of the U.S. Civil Code
Feedback / Explanation: One's right to use may not infringe on the rights of others to use and enjoy their property

Question: Surface rights, air rights and subsurface rights are
Feedback / Explanation: The bundle of real property rights also applies separately to the individual components of real estate: the air, the surface, and the subsurface. An owner can, for example, transfer subsurface rights without transferring air rights. Similarly, an owner can rent air space without encumbering surface or subsurface rights

Question: Which of the following terms refers to the rights of a property that abuts a stream or river?
Feedback / Explanation: Riparian rights concern properties abutting moving water such as streams and rivers

Question: What part of a non-navigable waterway does the owner of an abutting property own?
B: To the middle of the waterway
Feedback / Explanation: If the property abuts a non-navigable stream, the owner enjoys unrestricted use of the water and owns the land beneath the stream to the stream's midpoint

Question: What is the "Doctrine of Prior Appropriation?"
B: The right of government to confiscate land and improvements
C: A doctrine that gives the state control of water use and the water supply
D: A real estate tax applied to owners of water rights
Feedback / Explanation: Since water is a resource necessary for survival, some states -- particularly those where water is scarce -- have taken the legal position that the state owns and controls all bodies of water. Called the Doctrine of Prior Appropriation, this position requires that property owners obtain permits for use of water

Question: Which of the following is considered real property?
A: A tree growing on a parcel of land
B: A tree that has been cut down and is lying on a parcel of land
C: A tractor used to mow grass on a parcel of land
D: A prefabricated shed not yet assembled on a parcel of land
Feedback / Explanation: The primary criterion for distinguishing real from personal property is whether the item is permanently attached to the land or to structures attached to the land. For example, a tree growing in one's yard is an item of real property

Question: The overriding test of whether an item is a fixture or personal property is
A: how long it has been attached to the real property
B: its definition as one or the other in a sale or lease contract
C: how essential it is to the functioning of the property
D: how it was treated in previous transactions
Feedback / Explanation: If a person intended an article to be a fixture, even though the item is easily removable

Question: What is an emblement?
A: A piece of equipment affixed to the earth
B: A limited right to use personal property
C: A sign indicating a property boundary
D: A plant or crop that is considered personal property
Feedback / Explanation: Plants and crops requiring human intervention and labor are called emblements. Emblements, despite their attachment to land, are considered personal property

Question: An item can be converted from real to personal property and vice versa by means of which processes?
Feedback / Explanation: The classification may be changed by the process of conversion. Severance is the conversion of real property to personal property by detaching it from the real estate, such as by cutting down a tree, detaching a door from a shed, or removing an antenna from a roof. Affixing, or attachment, is the act of converting personal property to real property by attaching it to the real estate, such as by assembling a pile of bricks into a barbecue pit, or constructing a boat dock from wood planks

Question: The major sources of real estate law are legislation at federal, state and local level, and
Feedback / Explanation: Regulation takes the form of federal and state laws and regulations; county and local ordinances and codes; and court decisions in the judicial system

Question: What is the primary thrust of federal involvement in real estate law?
Feedback / Explanation: In regulating real property rights, the federal government is primarily concerned with broad standards of real property usage, natural disaster, land description, and discrimination

Question: Which level of government controls zoning laws?
Feedback / Explanation: County and local governments have the power to zone land

Question: A grocer temporarily installs special fruit and vegetable coolers in a leased grocery store in order to prevent spoilage. The coolers would be considered which of the following?
A: Trade fixtures that are real property
B: Trade fixtures that are personal property
C: Permanent fixtures that are real property
D: Permanent fixtures that are personal property
Feedback / Explanation: Trade fixtures are items of a tenant's personal property that the tenant has temporarily affixed to a landlord's real property in order to conduct business. Trade fixtures may be detached and removed before or upon surrender of the leased premises. Should the tenant fail to remove a trade fixture, it may become the property of the landlord through accession. Thereafter, the fixture is considered real property

Question: Under the doctrine of littoral rights, an owner claims ownership of all of the land underlying a lake where there are three other abutting property owners.  Which of the following is true?
A: The owner's claim is invalid, because the state owns the underlying land
B: The owner's claim is invalid, because the underlying land is shared equally with the other owners
C: The owner's claim is invalid, because he may only own underlying land to the middle of the lake
D: The owner's claim is valid, because the lake is navigable
Feedback / Explanation: The owner's claim is invalid, because the state owns the underlying land

Question: Which of the following scenarios represents an owners undivided fractional interest in a property?
A: A local municipality is condemning an 8-story building
B: Condominium owners allocate three floors as a designated parking space
C: A general contractor encumbered the property when the condominium owner failed to pay
D: A mortgagor conveys legal title to the mortgagee
Feedback / Explanation: The undivided interest represents an owners interest when there are two or more parties in a shared estate. It does not reflect the physical division of the property

Question: Which of the following represents the highest form of estate ownership?
Feedback / Explanation: Properties conveyed as Fee Simple Absolute estates are considered the highest form of ownership because the heirs can dispose of, enjoy, exclude, control, and possess the property generally without restriction

Question: Which of the following distinguishes a determinable estate from a condition subsequent estate?
A: A determinable estate focuses on the use of the estate
B: The dispute of a determinable estate is settled in court
C: The recipient of a determinable estate determines the use of the estate
D: The remainderman of the determinable estate inherits the property
Feedback / Explanation: In a fee simple defeasible estate, the use of the property is established by the owner through a determinable estate. Should the property not be used according to the owners instructions, the property will automatically revert back to the grantor

Question: Which of the following reflects an estate restricted by specific dates or duration?
Feedback / Explanation: A leasehold estate refers to rental properties, whereby a lessor transfers possession of a premises to a lessee for a definitive period of time

Question: Which of the following is a right provided to a life tenant?
Feedback / Explanation: The life tenant holds the rights of possession during a life estate and reverts to the original owner or to another designated individual upon death

Question: What type of leasehold estate does not have a definitive end date?
Feedback / Explanation: An estate at will continues indefinitely and terminates when either party gives sufficient notice or upon death

Question: Which of the following statements describes a circumstance that involves an owners use of homestead rights?
A: An owner sells the property to a neighbor
B: An owner is forced to sell his or her principal residence to satisfy unsecured creditors
C: An owner is selling the property by voluntary alienation
D: The grantor is creating a life estate for an elderly parent
Feedback / Explanation: The homestead right is a form of protection against unsatisfied creditors attempting to collect a debt

Question: How do fee simple estates differ from life estates?
B: Life estates transfer assets only between family members
C: All fee simple estates are conditional
D: Fee simple estates have fewer rights than life estates
Feedback / Explanation: Ownership rights in fee simple estates include the right to dispose of the property

Question: What type of leasehold allows a tenant to renew the terms automatically?
Feedback / Explanation: The lease automatically renews upon acceptance of rent from the tenant to the landlord

Question: Mr. Brady left farmland for his nephew, allowing him to grow any vegetation. The only rule imposed by Mr. Brady is prohibiting the use of any pesticides or chemicals added to the soil.  Unfortunately, his nephew used chemicals and other products to accelerate growth. Because there was a violation of the conditions of the fee simple defeasible estate, Mr. Brady wishes to repossess the farmland.  Which type of fee simple defeasible estate will allow Mr. Brady to repossess the property due to a violation of conditions?
Feedback / Explanation: Mr. Brady placed a condition to not use pesticides or chemicals.  Because this condition was violated, Mr. Brady may repossess the estate.  In a fee simple defeasible estate, condition subsequent, if any condition is violated, the previous owner may repossess the property. However, reversion of the estate is not automatic: the grantor must re-take physical possession within a certain time frame

Question: Charles created a life estate for his mother. Upon his mothers death, Charles will have his daughter Mary move into the condominium where his mother lived. Mary, who will have a future interest, is known as the
Feedback / Explanation: If a life estate names a third party to receive title to the property upon termination of the life estate, the party who has a future interest is called a remainderman

Question: Lisa grants a life estate for Caregiver Joanne, over the lifetime of Lisas mom. Upon the death of Lisas mom, Lisa grants her niece the right to receive the property. What type of conventional life estate did Lisa create?
Feedback / Explanation: The pur autre vie life estate extends over the lifetime of a third person, after which the property passes from the tenant holder to the original grantor (reversion) or a third party (remainderman)

Question: A 3-story residential property is being considered for eminent domain. The landlord has allowed the current tenants to reside until the property has been condemned. What type of a leasehold estate did both parties agree to?
Feedback / Explanation: Also known as tenancy at will, an estate at will has no definite expiration date or renewal cycle, so long as the rent is paid timely and other lease conditions are met

Question: Karen signed an estate for years with Draper Apartments. As the lessee, what type of rights will she have under this leasehold estate?
Feedback / Explanation: Leasehold tenants are entitled to possess and use the leased premises during the lease term in the manner prescribed in the lease

Question: The lessor and lessee signed a 1-year lease agreement.  Upon the expiration of the lease, the lessee failed to leave the apartment and continued to occupy the property without paying rent or obtaining the landlords consent. This rental situation is an example of which of the following?
Feedback / Explanation: An estate at sufferance occurs when the lessor and lessee have an agreement initially, but  the lessee continues to occupy the property after lease expiration without the consent of the landlord

Question: Municipalities have the right to enact ordinances and zoning laws for the public good within their jurisdiction.  This governmental power is known as
Feedback / Explanation: Police power, or the right of the local or county government to zone, is an enabling act whereby government powers are transferred to the local municipality to control land use for public good within the entitys jurisdiction

Question: A freehold estate of potentially unlimited duration is a
Feedback / Explanation: A freehold estate of potentially unlimited duration is a fee simple estate: an estate limited to the life of the owner is a life estate

Question: A legal life estate is created by
Feedback / Explanation: A legal life estate is created by state law as opposed to being created by a property owner's agreement. Provisions vary from state to state. The focus of a legal life estate is defining and protecting the property rights of surviving family members upon the death of the husband or wife

Question: The principal interests accompanying the legal life estate are the
A: remainderman, reversion and pur autre vie
D: homestead, dower and curtesy, and elective share
Feedback / Explanation: The principal interests conveyable in a legal life estate are the homestead, dower and curtesy, and elective share

Question: Which of the following statements regarding the general provisions of the homestead laws is true?
A: The homestead exemption is also known as the homestead tax exemption, which exempts a portion of the property's value from taxation
B: The homestead interest can be conveyed by one spouse; both spouses do not need to sign the deed conveying the homestead property
C: The homestead laws can be applied to any investment property that that is owned by a married couple
D: The homestead laws protect family members against losing their homes to general creditors attempting to collect on debts
Feedback / Explanation: A homestead is one's principal residence. Homestead laws protect family members against losing their homes to general creditors attempting to collect on debts

Question: If a tenant with a leasehold estate for years remains in possession of the leased premises after lease expiration, a(n) ___________ is created.
Feedback / Explanation: If a tenant with a leasehold estate for years remains in possession of the leased premises after lease expiration, a holdover lease is created. In the absence of a new lease agreement stating otherwise, the holdover tenancy becomes a periodic tenancy.

Question: Two people own a house, each having an undivided equal interest.  Which of the following best describes what they own?
A: Each owns fifty percent of the physical house and the land it rests on
B: Each owns one hundred percent of the physical house and the land it rests on
C: Each owns fifty percent of the estate represented by the real property, but no physical portion of the real property
D: Each owns one hundred percent of the estate represented by the real property and fifty percent of the physical house and the land it rests on
Feedback / Explanation: An undivided interest is an owner's interest in a property in which two or more parties share ownership. The terms "undivided" and "indivisible" signify that the owner's interest is in a fractional part of the entire estate, not in a physical portion of the real property itself. If two co-owners have an undivided equal interest, one owner may not lay claim to the northern half of the property for his or her exclusive use

Question: An interest in real estate that includes the right to possess is considered
Feedback / Explanation: Interests are principally distinguished by whether they include possession. If the interest-holder enjoys the right of possession, the party is considered to have an estate in land, or, familiarly an estate

Question: An interest in real estate that does not include the right to possess is considered
Feedback / Explanation: Interests are principally distinguished by whether they include possession. If a private interest-holder does not have the right to possess, the interest is an encumbrance

Question: The right to zone land and the right to acquire ownership of land by eminent domain are both examples of
Feedback / Explanation: Interests are principally distinguished by whether they include possession. If the interest-holder is not private, such as a government entity, and does not have the right to possess, the interest is some form of public interest

Question: If the duration of an owner's rights in an estate cannot be determined, the owner possesses
Feedback / Explanation: Freehold estates are ownership estates, and their duration cannot be determined; that is to say that the ownership rights may endure for a lifetime, for less than a lifetime, or for generations beyond the owner's lifetime

Question: The distinguishing feature of a leasehold estate is
A: ownership of an interest by a tenant
B: temporary ownership of the full bundle of rights in a property
C: unlimited ownership of one right in the bundle of rights in a property
D: duration limited by a lease term
Feedback / Explanation: A leasehold estate is distinguished by its specific duration, as represented by the lease term. Further, leasehold tenants only enjoy limited property rights: use; temporary possession, and limited exclusion

Question: Which of the following is a freehold tenant?
A: The owner of a freehold estate
B: The lessee in a leased property
C: The lessee of a freehold estate
D: Any owner of an estate in land
Feedback / Explanation: The owner of the freehold estate is the freehold tenant

Question: In what kind of estate does ownership revert to the grantor of the estate if usage fails to conform to a stated condition in the grant?
Feedback / Explanation: The defeasible fee estate is perpetual, provided the usage conforms to stated conditions

Question: Bob grants his sister Mary an estate for as long as she lives, but her heirs cannot inherit the estate.  What kind of estate is it?
Feedback / Explanation: An ordinary life estate ends with the death of the life estate owner and may pass back to the original owners or their heirs (reversion) or to a named third party (remainder)

Question: Homestead, dower and curtesy, and elective share are examples of
C: an estate created by an owner's agreement
Feedback / Explanation: The major forms of legal life estate are the homestead, dower and curtesy, and elective share

Question: To convey clear title in a dower and curtesy state,
A: both spouses must release their interest to the other spouse
B: both spouses must sign the conveyance
C: there must be two separate conveyances, each signed by one spouse
D: the head of household must sign the conveyance
Feedback / Explanation: To transfer property within dower and curtesy states, the husband (or wife) must obtain a release of the dower interest from the other spouse in order to convey clear title to another party. If both parties sign the conveyance, the dower right is automatically extinguished

Question: States that do not have dower and curtesy usually have either community property laws or
Feedback / Explanation: Dower and curtesy laws have been largely supplanted by community property laws and elective share laws

Question: Which of the following rights is conveyed with a leasehold estate?
A: The right to exclude everyone from the premises
B: The right to encumber the fee interest
C: The right to sell the premises
D: The right to possess and use the premises
Feedback / Explanation: Leasehold tenants are entitled to possess and use the leased premises during the lease term in the manner prescribed in the lease

Question: An estate from period-to-period will continue as long as
A: the tenant makes, and landlord accepts, regular rent payments
B: the term specified in the lease
C: the period is less than a year
D: the landlord has not given notice of termination
Feedback / Explanation: In an estate from period-to-period, also called a periodic tenancy, the tenancy period automatically renews as long as the tenant pays rent in a timely manner and the landlord accepts it. At the end of a tenancy period, if the landlord accepts another regular payment of rent, the leasehold is considered to be renewed for another period. A conveyance of leased property does not cancel a leasehold interest

Question: An estate at will
B: is terminated only if so stated in the lessee's last will and testament
C: terminates on the death of lessor or lessee
D: terminates on the date specified in the lease agreement
Feedback / Explanation: The estate at will, also called a tenancy at will, has no definite expiration date and hence no "renewal" cycle. The landlord and tenant agree that the tenancy will have no specified termination date, provided rent is paid on time and other lease conditions are met. For example, a son leases a house to his father and mother "forever," or until they want to move. The estate at will is terminated by proper notice, or by the death of either party

Question: If a tenant occupies a leased property without the consent of the landlord after a lease expires, the type of estate that results is called
Feedback / Explanation: In an estate at sufferance, a tenant occupies the premises without consent of the landlord or other legal agreement with the landlord. Usually such an estate involves a tenant who fails to vacate at the expiration of the lease, continuing occupancy without any right to do so

Question: An interest in real estate is best defined as ownership of
A: the full bundle of rights to real property
C: one or more of the bundle of rights to real property
D: the right to possession and use of real property
Feedback / Explanation: An interest in real estate is ownership of any combination of the bundle of sticks, including the rights to possess, use, transfer, encumber and exclude

Question: Encumbrances and police powers are
A: interests that do not include possession
Feedback / Explanation: An encumbrance enables a non-owning party to restrict the owner's bundle of rights. Government entities also have nonpossessory interests in real estate which act to control land use for the public good within the entity's jurisdiction. The prime example of public interest is police power

Question: What distinguishes a freehold estate from a leasehold estate?
A: A freehold includes the right to dispose or use
B: A leasehold endures only for a specific period of time
D: A leasehold is subject to government restrictions
Feedback / Explanation: Freehold estates are ownership estates, and their duration cannot be determined. A leasehold endures only for a specific period of time

Question: The highest form of ownership interest one can acquire in real estate is the
Feedback / Explanation: The fee simple freehold estate is the highest form of ownership interest one can acquire in real estate. It includes the complete bundle of rights, and the tenancy is unlimited, with certain exceptions. The fee simple absolute estate is a perpetual estate that is not conditioned by stipulated or restricted uses

Question: The distinguishing feature of a defeasible fee simple estate is that
A: it can be passed on to heirs
B: it has no restrictions on use
C: the estate may revert to a grantor or heirs if the prescribed use changes
Feedback / Explanation: The defeasible fee estate is perpetual, provided the usage conforms to stated conditions. Essential characteristics are: the property must be used for a certain purpose or under certain conditions; and, if the use changes or if prohibited conditions are present, the estate reverts to the previous grantor of the estate

Question: Upon the death of the owner, a life estate passes to
A: the original owner or other named person
Feedback / Explanation: Upon the death of the owner or other named individual, the estate passes to the original owner or another named party

Question: How is a conventional life estate created?
A: It happens automatically when title transfers unless a fee simple is specifically claimed
B: A fee simple owner grants the life estate to a life tenant
C: It is created by judicial action
D: It is created by a statutory period of adverse possession
Feedback / Explanation: A conventional life estate is created by grant from a fee simple property owner to the grantee, the life tenant

Question: What distinguishes a pur autre vie life estate from an ordinary life estate?
A: The pur autre vie estate endures only for the lifetime of the grantor
B: The pur autre vie estate endures only for the lifetime of the grantee
C: The pur autre vie estate endures only for the lifetime of a person other than the grantee
D: The pur autre vie estate cannot revert to the grantor
Feedback / Explanation: A pur autre vie life estate endures over the lifetime of a third person, after which the property passes from the tenant holder to the original grantor (reversion) or a third party (remainderman)

Question: Which of the following life estates is created by someone other than the owner?
Feedback / Explanation: A legal life estate is created by state law as opposed to being created by a property owner's agreement

Question: Which of the following is true of a homestead?
A: A homestead interest cannot be conveyed by one spouse
B: A homestead interest cannot be passed to the children of the head of household
C: A homestead interest is a form of conventional life estate
D: A homestead is a primary or secondary residence occupied by a family
Feedback / Explanation: A homestead is one's principal residence. Homestead laws protect family members against losing their homes to general creditors attempting to collect on debts. Homestead laws generally provide that: the homestead interest cannot be conveyed by one spouse; both spouses must sign the deed conveying homestead property

Question: Dower refers to
A: joint tenancy of husband and wife
B: a wife's life estate interest in her husband's property
D: a child's life estate interest in his or her parents' homestead
Feedback / Explanation: Dower refers to a wife's life estate interest in the husband's property. When the husband dies, the wife can make a claim to portions of the decedent's property. Curtesy is the identical right enjoyed by the husband in a deceased wife's property. Property acquired under dower laws is owned by the surviving spouse for the duration of his or her lifetime

Question: Which of the following is an illustration of the legal concept of elective share?
A: A surviving spouse places a lien on a debtor's property
B: A widow who was excluded from a will makes a claim to a portion of the couple's principal residence
C: A spouse who loses her home because of her husband's gambling debt sues in court for exemption from the debt
D: A widower whose spouse died without a will sues to change the provisions of the will
Feedback / Explanation: Elective share is a state-level statute enabling a surviving spouse to make a minimum claim to the deceased spouse's real and personal property in place of the provisions for such property in the decedent's will

Question: A one-year lease on a house has expired, but the tenant continues sending monthly rent checks to the owner, and the owner accepts them. What kind of leasehold estate exists?
Feedback / Explanation: In an estate from period-to-period, also called a periodic tenancy, the tenancy period automatically renews for an indefinite period of time, subject to timely payment of rent. At the end of a tenancy period, if the landlord accepts another regular payment of rent, the leasehold is considered to be renewed for another period

Question: If a lien covers all properties of the debtor in that county where it is recorded, it is known as:
Feedback / Explanation: General Liens applies to and burdens all of the owner's real property, but only in the county of recording. [Examples include tax liens or liens resulting from a court judgment.]. Therefore a judgment lien is not the answer because it is one of many other types of general liens.

Question: Generally, construction onto the property of another, like a wall fence, building, etc. is known as what?
Feedback / Explanation: When you think of the word encroachment you think of somebody stepping over a line from their side on to yours. An encroachment is the physical intrusion of a structure or improvement on the land of another. Examples can also include a fence or driveway over the property line. Remember your property line extends upwards to the sky therefore a tree hanging over on to your property is encroachment as well. An encroachment can be found by a survey. It is a form of trespass, therefore when an encroachment occurs you may be able to sue your neighbor for trespassing.

Question: Which of the following would be considered real property?
Feedback / Explanation: Real property is that category of property that is immovable by law. Examples include load bearing walls, stock in a mutual water company, and minerals.

Question: Personal property can be:
A: turned into real property, alienated, and/or hypothecated.
Feedback / Explanation: Personal property are items that are generally moveable and do not go with the real estate. Personal property can be turned into real property (such as in a fixture) it can be alienated (you can get rid of it) and it can be hypothecated (pledged as collateral to secure a loan).

Question: Which of the following would likely be defined as a fixture?
B: A built-in stove in a mobile home
Feedback / Explanation: Fixtures are objects attached to real property or incorporated into the land which are now considered real property. To determine whether or not an item of Personal Property has become a Fixture, use the Maria test: Method, Adaptability, Relationship, Intention, Agreement. The size, cost and time of attachment are NOT factors. Mobile homes are generally not considered real property because they're not attached to the land, so a stove in a mobile home is not usually a fixture.

Question: Which of the following would be most likely found in a business office?
A: None of the other options are correct
Feedback / Explanation: Trade Fixtures are items that are attached to the property, but they are not considered real property because they are in a business office. They can leave with the person as they do not stay with the property. Trade fixtures are not appurtenant to the land because they are personal property. Always keep in mind to choose the best answer.

Question: Zena granted the property to Ungrilda, but because she knew that Ungrilda had a drinking problem she told her that there was a condition. The condition was that the property would be forfeited if alcohol is sold on the premise. Ungrilda would have which of the following estates?
Feedback / Explanation: A Fee Simple Defeasible puts conditions on the use of a property. For example, an owner could lose title if the condition that no alcohol is sold on the property is violated.

Question: The holder of a life estate, based on her own life, may do all of the following, except:
Feedback / Explanation: A Life Estate is a type of Freehold Estate which has a duration measured by the length of someone's life, after which the estate passes to a reversionary or remainder interest-holder. A life estate measured by the holder's life cannot be devised, but it can be borrowed against, leased or sold (though the purchaser will lose any rights when the life-estate holder dies and the property passes to the reversionary or remainder interest-holder).

Question: Placing two or more contiguous units under common ownership, for the purpose of increasing the value per unit, is known as:
Feedback / Explanation: Plottage is the common ownership of two or more units or lots next to each other which makes the value of the new combined property greater than the values of each individual lot added together. Assemblage is the process by which plottage occurs.

Question: A situation in which the tenant's lease has expired, the tenant is no longer paying rent and is living on the property without permission is known as:
Feedback / Explanation: In an estate at sufferance, the tenancy is by a lessee who lawfully occupies the landlord's property, but then continues to occupy the property improperly after his rights have expired and without permission of the landlord. Think of it this way: The landlord and the estate are SUFFERING because rent is not being paid. The other types of estates mentioned here are actual lease agreements, but this is a situation of one party being unwilling to abide by the terms of any agreements previously reached.

Question: The right-of-way to access telephone lines is a common example of:
Feedback / Explanation: An easement in gross grants a company or individual limited rights to access another's property for a specific purpose. In this case installing, maintaining and repairing telephone lines.

Question: Title held by a husband and wife may include all of the following forms of ownership EXCEPT:
Feedback / Explanation: Severalty means sole ownership and, other than forming a corporation, spouses can only hold property in common through some form of joint ownership.

Question: Mary owns one parcel of land and is trying to show that exceptional circumstances exist that justify using the land in a way that is prohibited by current zoning laws. Her intended use would not be detrimental to the public. She would probably petition the planning commission for a:
Feedback / Explanation: A petition for variance is a method by which a landowner can try to show that unique circumstances exist and that his land should be allowed an exception to present zoning laws. Variance is done lot by lot.

Question: The difference between a judgment lien and a mechanic's lien is:
A: mechanic's liens are created by statute.
B: mechanic's liens may take priority earlier than the date they are recorded.
D: judgment liens are not enforceable until recorded.
Feedback / Explanation: Mechanic's liens take priority as of the first day of construction, and may even take priority earlier than their date of recording.

Question: Unless otherwise specified, the rights to minerals which lie beneath the surface of land:
A: Cannot be conveyed apart from the real estate surrounding the minerals.
B: Are kept by the original grantor;
C: Are automatically transferred with the sale of the real property;
Feedback / Explanation: Appurtenances are said to "Run with the Land" and they require no separate conveyance. Appurtenances include Easements, Stock in a Mutual Water Company, Covenants, and Minerals. They are considered real property.

Question: Real estate brokers can have difficulty with personal property because ownership rights and identification are often difficult to determine. This is because personal property can:
A: be alienated, hypothecated and can become real property.
Feedback / Explanation: Personal Property, also known as "chattel", is a category of property that is generally movable and can be hypothecated (pledged as collateral), alienated (transfer of ownership) and become real property (as a fixture).

Question: "Of indefinite duration" is a characteristic which is particularly descriptive of an:
Feedback / Explanation: Fee Simple Estate: Also referred to as an "Estate of Inheritance", a Fee Simple Estate is one type of Freehold Estate, and as such, is indefinite in duration. A Fee Simple Estate can also be sold or inherited, and is not free of Encumbrances. A Fee Simple Absolute is the most interest that one can hold in land.

Question: A freehold estate consists of:
C: An estate from period to period;
Feedback / Explanation: Freehold estate is an estate in which ownership is for an indeterminate length of time, that would be "fee estate"; the other options are all examples of less than freehold estates.

Question: The deed restrictions on a subdivision said that the lots must contain a minimum of 15,000 square feet structure. The zoning restrictions said that the lots must contain a minimum of 10,000 square feet structure. Which would prevail in this situation?
Feedback / Explanation: The higher standard of restrictions will usually prevail. For example, if deed restrictions in a subdivision required that each lot contain a minimum 15,000 square feet structure, and the applicable zoning laws required that each lot contain a minimum 10,000 square foot structure, the deed restrictions would prevail. With things like this, you usually go with the option that is more restrictive. NOTE - This question asks about the MINIMUM square footage of a structure, so the more restrictive number is 15,000 sq. ft. If the question asked about the MAXIMUM square footage of a structure, the more restrictive number would be 10,000 sq. ft.

Question: When the rent charged is based on the gross sales of a business, the lease is correctly termed:
Feedback / Explanation: Percentage Leases: In this type of lease, the amount of rent paid by the lessee is determined by calculating a percentage based on the gross receipts of the lessee's business (i.e., a commercial parking lot).

Question: Which of the following is not a characteristic of a fee simple absolute title?
A: It is transferable with or without consideration
C: It is always free of encumbrances
D: It is transferable by will or intestate
Feedback / Explanation: Be careful of words like "always" on your exam. There is usually an exception to just about every rule. Also referred to as an "Estate of Inheritance", a Fee Simple Estate is one type of freehold estate and is indefinite in duration. A fee simple estate can also be sold or inherited, and is not free of encumbrances  remember that there are always taxes, and taxes are an encumbrance. A fee simple absolute is the most interest that one can hold in land.

Question: A family rents a vacation home from July 5, 2020 to January 31, 2021. Their tenancy would properly be classified as:
Feedback / Explanation: An estate for years is a lease for any fixed period of time, for example a lease that commences on July 5th, 2020 and terminates on January 31, 2021. An estate for years already has a fixed end date when the lease begins  this separates it from a periodic tenancy, in which the lease automatically renews at the end of each agreed-upon period (usually from 1 week to 1 year) until either the tenant or landlord gives notice of termination.

Question: As used in real property law, "tenancy" most nearly means:
A: The mode or method of holding title to real property by an owner.
B: A tenant who has acted in excess of their rights.
C: The obstinancy of a holdover tenant.
Feedback / Explanation: Title vesting, also referred to as "Tenancy", simply refers to the method or mode of holding title to real property.

Question: Mr. Smith owns real property in severalty. This term is used to legally describe ownership by:
Feedback / Explanation: Ownership in severalty is a type of ownership, also known as separate ownership by one individual (or one corporation).

Question: If five people own property as joint tenants, which of the following is true?
A: Each co-owner has a separate legal title to his undivided interest
B: They do not need to own equal interest in the property
C: If one of the owner dies, his heirs become tenants in common with the surviving co-owners
D: There is still only one title to the whole property
Feedback / Explanation: Joint Tenancy can be summarized with the word unity: unity of Time, Title, Interest and Possession. The unity of Title means that no matter how many joint tenants there are, there is still only one title to the entire property. The unity of Time means that all co-owners must have received their interest in the property at the same time, the unity of Interest means that all co-owners must have an equal interest (5 owners would mean each owner has 20% interest) and the unity of Possession means that each owner has an equal right to possess the property as the other owners.

Question: When the preliminary title report reveals the existence of an easement on the property, it indicates that the easement is a(n):
Feedback / Explanation: Anything that limits a person's use of a property is an encumbrance. Easements limit use in that they generally prohibit any kind of permanent structure on the area in question. For example, if a homeowner wanted to build a swimming pool in an area of his back yard and the local sewer company had an easement for pipes running under that area, he would have to find another location for his pool, even if it was not as desirable.

Question: All of the following are examples of police power EXCEPT:
Feedback / Explanation: Restrictive covenants are private matters established and enforced by residents on a voluntary basis. Police powers, on the other hand, are imposed by governments and impact all affected persons.

Question: In determining whether an item is a fixture, which of the following would not be considered?
B: intention of the parties when the fixture was installed
Feedback / Explanation: Please be aware of words like "not" or "except" in the question. Fixtures are objects that are attached to real property or incorporated into the land which are now considered real property; in determining whether or not an item of Personal Property has become a Fixture. Use the Maria test (Method Adaptability Relationship Intention Agreement)  the size, cost and time of attachment are NOT factors.

Question: An item which was personal property but has been attached to and become part of the real property is known as:
Feedback / Explanation: A fixture is personal property that is now considered real property. For example, a new cooktop is personal property until it is installed, at which point it becomes a fixture. The way to test if something is a fixture is to use the acronym MARIA. MARIA stands for Method, Adaptability, Relationship, Intention and Agreement.

Question: Which of the following is not required for obtaining an easement by prescription?
A: A hostile use which is adverse to the true owner
B: Open notorious use which is continuous
