Feedback / Explanation: Easement by Prescription obtained by open, notorious, hostile, and continuous use. Confrontation with the owner is not a factor. Easement by prescription can be terminated for non-use.

Question: Any claim or liability attached to a property is called:
Feedback / Explanation: Remember that an encumbrance is any claim against a property. Restrictions, liens, and buyer's claims (also known as a vendee's lien) are all FORMS of encumbrances.

Question: In an appurtenant easement, the party that benefits is known as the:
Feedback / Explanation: The person whose land is benefited by the easement, and is the DOMINATING PARTY, is considered the dominant tenement. The servient tenement is the opposite of this. Leaser and lessee involve lease agreements, and have nothing to do with easements.

Question: The rights of a landowner to use waters of an adjacent stream or river are known as:
Feedback / Explanation: While both riparian and littoral rights have to do with water, remember that riparian rights have to do with FLOWING bodies of water, such as rivers. (River and Riparian both start with 'r'.) Zoning and seisin deal with land control.

Question: Which of the following methods of creating an easement would result in an easement which could most easily be terminated for non-use?
B: A valid quitclaim deed from the current owner
Feedback / Explanation: Easement by Prescription (requires open, notorious, hostile, and continuous use). Confrontation with the owner is not a factor. Easement by prescription can be terminated for non-use.

Question: Dan sold his land which had an easement appurtenant for a road. The deed which the buyer received contained an adequate description of the land, but made no reference to the easement. The buyer:
A: Loses the easement to the servient tenement
B: Has the same right to the easement as the seller did
D: Takes title to property which is landlocked
Feedback / Explanation: An appurtenant easement is an easement that is annexed to the ownership of one parcel and allows the owner the use of the neighbor's land. The buyer has the same rights as the seller.

Question: Private restrictions on real property can be written either as a condition or a covenant. Which of the following is most correct concerning such restrictions?
A: Violation of covenants can result in penalties which are more severe and can result in loss of money only.
B: Violation of a condition can result in penalties which are more severe and can result in loss of title.
C: They become public restrictions upon recording.
D: Violations of such penalties are a criminal offense.
Feedback / Explanation: When private restrictions take the form of conditions in written agreements, any breach of such conditions can result in more severe penalties than if they were in the form of a covenant. Often violations result in a loss of title, such as with a fee simple defeasible estate.

Question: Following proper action by the authorities, the zoning on a vacant lot was changed from "commercial" to "residential." Such an action is called:
Feedback / Explanation: Down zoning refers to zoning changes that convert commercially zoned properties into ones zoned for residential use.

Question: Which of the following would be defined as a fixture in the law?
Feedback / Explanation: Fixtures are objects attached to real property or incorporated into the land which are now considered real property. Use MARIA: Method, Adaptability, Relationship, Intention, Agreement.

Question: Re-zoning a section of town and changing its lawful use for new development from commercial to residential use is known as:
Feedback / Explanation: Down zoning usually reduces the economic value of the property involved.

Question: An estate in real property:
A: Will always run on into perpetuity.
B: Must only carry the right to immediate possession.
C: May exist along with other estates in the same property.
D: Can come into being only through a grant.
Feedback / Explanation: An estate in real property may exist along with other estates in the same property.

Question: A tenant rented an apartment, signing a 15-month lease. After the lease expired, the tenant stayed in the apartment without the landlord's permission. What kind of leasehold does the tenant have?
Feedback / Explanation: Holdover tenancy is a tenancy whereby a lessee retains possession of leased property after the lease has expired without the landlord's permission.

Question: Which of the following terms BEST describes personal property that, by its attachment to real property, is regarded as real estate?
Feedback / Explanation: Fixtures are objects attached to real property or incorporated into the land and which are now considered real property. Use MARIA.

Question: Keith and Ralph bought a house as tenants in common. If Keith dies, which of the following statements about ownership of the house is CORRECT?
A: Ralph holds life estate ownership in Keith's share of the property.
B: Ralph holds fee simple ownership in Keith's share of the property.
C: Ralph retains his interest in the property and Keith's share goes to his heirs.
D: It automatically becomes tenancy in severalty.
Feedback / Explanation: There is no right of survivorship when property is owned as tenants in common. Keith's share passes to his heirs.

Question: When a husband and wife take title to a property as tenants in common, they have elected a form of ownership that allows for:
A: the option of unequal shares of ownership
C: everything to be equal as per ownership of the property
Feedback / Explanation: Tenancy in common allows unequal shares. Unity of Possession is required; no automatic right of survivorship.

Question: In a condominium apartment project, the BEST term to describe swimming pools and laundry rooms for the use of unit owners is:
Feedback / Explanation: Common elements are portions of a condominium development owned in common by unit owners.

Question: Nelson owned two acres of land. He sold one acre to Andy and reserved for himself an appurtenant easement over Andy's land for ingress and egress. Andy's land:
A: can be cleared of the easement when Nelson sells the withheld acre to a third party.
B: is subject to an easement in gross.
Feedback / Explanation: Andy's land interest is the one burdened by the easement, making it the servient property.

Question: Ginny owns 50 acres of land with 500 feet of frontage on a desirable recreational lake. She wishes to subdivide the parcel into sellable lots, but she wants to retain control over the lake frontage while allowing lot owners to have access to the lake. Which of the following types of access rights would provide the greatest protection for a prospective purchaser?
Feedback / Explanation: Appurtenant easements afford the most protection since they pass with the property.

Question: Many states determine the order of water rights according to which users of the water hold a recorded beneficial use permit. This allocation of water rights is determined by:
Feedback / Explanation: In water-scarce states, the doctrine of prior appropriation applies: first in time, first in right.

Question: The type of lease that is for an indefinite amount of time and may be cancelled by the landlord or tenant at any time is known as:
D: an estate from period to period.
Feedback / Explanation: An estate at will continues indefinitely and may be terminated by either party.

Question: The local utility company dug up Bob's garden to install a natural gas line. The company claimed it had a valid easement and proved it through the county records. Bob claimed the easement was not valid because he did not know about it. The easement:
A: Was an appurtenant easement owned by the utility company.
B: Was not valid because Bob was not informed of its existence when he purchased the property.
C: Was valid even though the owner did not know about it.
D: Was not valid because it had not been used during the entire time that Bob owned the property.
Feedback / Explanation: Easements grant access and attach to the property even if the owner is unaware.

Question: Peter owned two acres of land. He sold one acre to Chaim and reserved for himself an appurtenant easement over Chaim's land for ingress and egress. Chaim's land:
A: Can be cleared of the easement when Peter sells the withheld acre to a third party.
B: Is subject to an easement in gross.
Feedback / Explanation: Chaim's land interest is burdened by the easement; it is the servient property.

Question: Leslie and Vivian bought a store building and took title as joint tenants. Vivian died testate. Leslie now owns the store:
B: as a joint tenant with rights of survivorship.
D: as a tenant in common with Vivian's heirs.
Feedback / Explanation: Joint tenancy means that upon death, the surviving tenant owns the property in severalty (sole ownership).

Question: Andrew lives in an apartment building. The land and structures are owned by a corporation, with one mortgage loan covering the entire property. Like the other residents, Andrew owns stock in the corporation and has a lease on his apartment. This type of ownership is called a(n):
Feedback / Explanation: Cooperative ownership involves stock in a corporation that entitles the holder to lease a unit.

Question: In a gift of a parcel of real estate, one of the two owners was given an undivided 60 percent interest and the other received an undivided 40 percent interest. The two owners hold their interests as what?
Feedback / Explanation: Because the interests were acquired as a gift and not simultaneously with survivorship intent, they are tenants in common.

Question: Which of the following liens does not need to be recorded to be valid?
Feedback / Explanation: Liens by taxing authorities do not need to be recorded.

Question: A tenant's lease has expired, but the tenant did not vacate the premises or negotiate a renewal lease. The landlord has declared that he does not want the tenant to remain in the building. This type of occupancy is referred to as an estate:
Feedback / Explanation: Estate at sufferance occurs when a tenant stays beyond the lease term without permission.

Question: The right to control one's property includes all of the following EXCEPT:
A: the right to enjoy pride of ownership.
B: the right to invite people on the property for a political fund-raiser.
C: the right to erect "no trespassing" signs.
D: the right to exclude the utilities meter reader.
Feedback / Explanation: Utility companies are granted easements to enter property; owners cannot exclude them.

Question: Which of the following is/are considered to be personal property?
Feedback / Explanation: Furniture, rugs, lamps, and other portable items that are not attached constitute personal property.

Question: The owner of a condominium unit learns that a neighbor has failed to pay his real estate taxes. If this neighbor does not pay the taxes:
A: a lien can be filed against the condominium, including all of the units.
B: a lien can be filed against all the common areas of the condominium.
C: a lien can be filed against the neighbor's unit and his percentage of the common elements.
D: the taxing authority can order the condominium to be dissolved.
Feedback / Explanation: A lien is filed against the individual unit and that owner's share of the common elements.

Question: Which of the following is an encumbrance?
B: All of the other options are correct
Feedback / Explanation: Encumbrances include anything affecting or limiting title: mortgages, liens, easements, leases, restrictions.

Question: Needing to complete a route for the new highway the state tries to buy Jones' ranch. He refuses to sell though. The state may go to court and ask that his ranch be condemned, allowing the land to be purchased. This would be an example of the state using its right of:
Feedback / Explanation: Eminent domain is the state's right to take land for public use. The government's right of eminent domain is enforced through condemnation, which results in just compensation being paid to the landowner whose land is being taken.

Question: One example of an easement in gross is the right of:
A: Utility companies to access a property in order to maintain wires or pipes
B: Owners to use the neighbor's backyard for their own recreation
C: Owners to cut across their neighbor's property
D: A roofing company working on repairs to store some of their equipment in the neighbor's yard
Feedback / Explanation: Public utility companies are given access over properties for the purpose of maintenance and repair of necessary lines, pipes, etc.

Question: The holder of a life estate can do all of the following EXCEPT:
Feedback / Explanation: A life estate only exists for the life of its owner or the life of some other designated person. NOTE: A life estate is OWNERSHIP that is based on the life of someone. They hold the full bundle of rights for as long as they are alive. If this were a life estate that was based on the life of another, it would have been called a life estate pur autre vie.

Question: Bill owns an easement in Joe's property/land. Bill is not a property owner. What kind of easement does Bill have?
Feedback / Explanation: An easement in gross is the limited right of one person (or party) to use another's land (servient estate) when such right is not created for the benefit of any land owned by the owner of the easement. In this case there is no dominant estate because the easement attaches personally to the owner, not a parcel of land.

Question: Freehold estate includes:
Feedback / Explanation: A freehold estate is an estate in which you have exclusive rights to enjoy the possession of a property for an undefined length of time. In contrast, a less than freehold estate is held for a fixed, defined period. One type of freehold estate is a life estate. The other options are all examples of a less than freehold estate.

Question: Under a lease, the leasehold interest lies in the:
Feedback / Explanation: A leasehold interest is the right to exclusive possession and use of real property for a fixed period of time held by the lessee (tenant).

Question: A, B, and C are co-owners of real property in joint tenancy. C sells his interest to D and, shortly thereafter, C dies. If C had also executed a will, leaving all of his property to his sole heir (whom we shall call E), title to the property would vest as:
A: A and B as joint tenants, together with E as a tenant in common
B: A and B as joint tenants, together with D as a tenant in common
C: A, B and E as joint tenants
D: A, B, and D as tenants in common
Feedback / Explanation: It is the right of any joint tenant to sell his share during ownership. In the example, the sale created a tenancy in common between D and the other joint tenants. The death of C had no effect as he died after the transfer, and E is irrelevant because C cannot will a joint tenancy share.

Question: Which of the following does not "go with the land"?
Feedback / Explanation: An easement in gross is held by a person or entity, not a property, so it does not "go with the land". Typically an easement is used to go to another property. With an easement in gross, there is no other property to go to. Therefore it would not go with the land because there is no land.

Question: The four unities of joint tenancy are
Feedback / Explanation: The four unities to a joint tenancy are: Time, Title, Interest and Possession. "TTIP"

Question: All of the following statements are true about zoning EXCEPT that:
A: zoning laws are concerned with regulating property values.
C: violations of zoning ordinances may render title unmarketable.
D: zoning laws are generally enforced through building permits.
Feedback / Explanation: Zoning laws are NOT retroactive. This means that if zoning changes, any existing properties are generally "grandfathered" in under a non-conforming use permit.

Question: A judgment will be most effective in the county where the:
Feedback / Explanation: A judgment has no legal effect until recorded, and then only to the extent it is recorded in a county in which the defendant owns real property.

Question: What does the term "short rate" refer to?
Feedback / Explanation: A higher periodic rate charged for a shorter term than that originally contracted. The increased premium charged by an insurance company on early cancellation of a policy to compensate the insurer for the fact that the original rate charged was calculated on the full period of the policy.

Question: a recorded deed:
B: all of the other options are correct
Feedback / Explanation: Delivery presumes acknowledgement and gives constructive notice. Recording establishes presumption of delivery.

Question: The four unities of joint tenancy are:
Feedback / Explanation: The four unities to a joint tenancy are: Time, Title, Interest and Possession. "TTIP" is an easy acronym to remember for these.

Question: If one uses the words "of definite duration" to describe a lease, it is most likely a(n):
Feedback / Explanation: An estate for years always will have a definite termination date which makes it a definite duration time. For example, when you see two dates such as January 5 to March 4th, that is an estate for years.

Question: Two men are co-owners in fee simple of a small office building. One of the men dies intestate and leaves nothing to be distributed to his heirs. The surviving owner is neither related to the deceased owner nor his creditor. What would explain how the surviving owner acquired the deceased owner's interest in the office building?
Feedback / Explanation: Upon the death of an owner in a joint tenancy, all remaining interests do not pass to the heirs of the deceased owner or according to the will but to the surviving joint tenant or tenants. Reversionary rights and remainder interests refer to the future right to an estate in real property. Adverse possession occurs when a person acquires title to another's property by means of open, hostile, and continuous possession over time.

Question: Property taxes levied on a property owner to pay for installation of sidewalks or sewers are called:
Feedback / Explanation: Special assessments are taxes levied on real estate to fund public improvements. General property taxes are taxes on real or personal property levied by a government taxing authority. Special excise taxes are direct taxes on services performed or income received, such as license fees and sales taxes. Real estate taxes are also known as ad valorem taxes, taxes based on the value of the specific property being taxed.

Question: A property owner who has the legal right to cross over a neighbor's land holds a(n)
Feedback / Explanation: An easement is a right of use or passage. An easement is not an estate in land, as it does not create any right of ownership. An easement is not an exercise of the police power of the state. An easement is not an encroachment, which is an illegal extension of a structure onto another's property.

Question: A license is an example of a(n)
Feedback / Explanation: A license is a personal and temporary right to use another's property. A license can be canceled or revoked at any time. An encroachment occurs when a building or part of a structure extends illegally onto another's property. An appurtenant easement permits the owner of one parcel to use an adjoining neighbor's parcel. A restriction limits the ways property owners may use their property.

Question: A property owner deeds a parcel of his property to a town "for recreational purposes only." The deed conveys to the town a
Feedback / Explanation: The deed conveys a fee simple defeasible estate which includes a specific condition on the use of the parcel. If the town uses the parcel for any purpose other than for recreational use, the property owner can take possession of the property through legal action. Holder of a leasehold estate has no ownership only possession. A fee simple absolute estate has no restrictions on the use of the property. A life estate is limited in duration to the life of the owner of the estate or to the life of some other designated person.

Question: A tax lien on a property can hinder the sale of the property because it is a(n):
C: general lien and will attach to the real and personal property.
D: monetary charge that the purchaser must satisfy.
Feedback / Explanation: An encumbrance is a claim, charge, or liability that attaches to real estate. It is a right or interest held by someone else other than the fee owner of the property that affects the title. A tax lien is a specific lien attached to the real property when real estate property taxes are not paid by the owner. It is not an encroachment, which is an illegal extension of a building or structure onto another's property. A purchaser is not required to satisfy a tax lien unless agreed to by the purchaser in the purchase contract.

Question: In receiving a gift of a parcel of real estate, one of the two new owners was given an undivided 60% share, and the other received an undivided 40% share. They now hold title as
Feedback / Explanation: Tenants in common hold property with undivided fractional interests, and the shares do not have to be equal. In a joint tenancy, each owner holds equal shares and interests to the property. Community property consists of personal or real property acquired by either party in a marriage and belonging to both parties to the marriage. In a cooperative, owners own shares in a corporation, partnership or trust which owns a property, with each owner holding a proprietary lease and the right to occupy the unit.

Question: Sally conveys a life estate to Scott for his lifetime and designates Bill as the person to whom the property will pass when the life estate ends. When Scott dies, what happens to the property?
A: The property reverts back to the original property owner.
B: The property passes to Scott's heirs.
C: Bill automatically becomes the fee simple owner of the property.
D: Sally must designate a remainderman for the property.
Feedback / Explanation: The property owner named Bill as the remainderman, the one to whom the property would pass upon the death of Scott. On Scott's death, the life estate ends, and the property automatically passes to Bill. If the property owner does not choose a remainderman when conveying the life estate to Scott, ownership reverts back to the original property owner or to her heirs.

Question: What is the difference between a general lien and a specific lien?
A: A general lien affects all of a debtor's property, while a specific lien affects only a certain piece of property.
B: A specific lien covers real estate, while a general lien covers personal property.
C: A general lien cannot be enforced in court, while a specific lien can be enforced.
D: A specific lien is held by one person, while a general lien is held by at least two people.
Feedback / Explanation: A general lien affects all of a debtor's property, both real and personal. A specific lien affects only a particular piece of real or personal property. Both general and specific liens may be enforced in court.

Question: Which lien affects all real and personal property of a debtor?
Feedback / Explanation: A general lien attaches to all of a debtor's real and personal property as opposed to a specific lien, which affects only a particular piece of real or personal property. A voluntary lien, such as a mortgage, is placed on a property with the consent of the owner. An involuntary lien is imposed upon a property without the consent of the owner.

Question: One major difference between joint tenancy and tenancy in common is:
A: joint tenancy requires all ownership rights to be acquired at the same time.
B: tenants in common have a separate legal title, which allows them to have divided interests.
C: tenants in common have survivor rights for each owner.
D: joint tenants have equal rights of possession, while tenants in common do not.
Feedback / Explanation: Joint tenancy (which offers the right of survivorship) requires PITT- possession, interest and title must all occur at the same time. Tenants in common owners have equal rights of possession but may have unequal rights of ownership. All owners, regardless of how ownership is taken, share one deed which conveys legal title.

Question: Which of the following best describes fee simple absolute estate?
A: It is the maximum estate in land.
B: The duration is dependent upon the language of the deed.
C: It will have the maximum rights unless a deed condition limits.
D: It is the maximum estate in land but lasts only for the duration of the property ownership.
Feedback / Explanation: Fee simple absolute estates, which have the largest (maximum) bundle of rights, are typically transferred from one owner to another when title is passed so they continue forever- there is no time limit to the duration of property ownership. A deed condition would create a fee simple defeasible or qualified estate.

Question: An ownership interest that is based on occupancy during a specified period of time, is a:
Feedback / Explanation: A real property interest with the right to use the facilities for a certain period of time is called a time-share. While a time-share may be sold as a leasehold or freehold interest, time-share intervals are usually shorter intervals of time, typically weekly intervals. A leasehold interest occurs when a tenant leases a property from an owner but usually for an extended longer period of time (such as one year). A condominium owner holds a fee simple absolute ownership to the unit and owns the common elements as tenants in common with the other owners. In a cooperative, tenants own shares in a corporation, partnership or trust that holds title to the building, with tenants having the right to occupy their own units but do not have a deed.

Question: Two friends bought a store building and took title as joint tenants. One woman died testate. The surviving woman now owns the store:
A: as a tenant in common with the dead woman's heirs.
C: as a joint tenant with rights of survivorship.
Feedback / Explanation: Joint tenancy includes the right to survivorship, which means the property passes to the surviving owner(s) upon the death of one tenant. In this case, the women took title as joint tenants. When one woman died, her share passed to the other owner who now owns the property in severalty.

Question: Which of the following about a condominium is FALSE?
A: Each unit owner owns the common elements as tenants in common with all other unit owners
B: Each owner has a proprietary lease with the association for their own unit
C: Each owner receives a separate real estate tax statement
D: Each owner must carry their own property insurance
Feedback / Explanation: In a condominium, each owner has fee simple title to the unit while owners of cooperatives have proprietary leases. In a condominium, each owner owns the common elements as tenants in common with all other unit owners. Each owner receives a separate real estate tax statement. Each owner of a condominium unit must carry their own property casualty insurance on the individual unit based upon the condominium declaration. The association carries insurance for the common elements. NOTE - This question asks "Which of the following statements... is FALSE" which means it's looking for a false answer, and it's false that each owner of a condominium has a proprietary lease.

Question: When a company furnishes materials for the construction of a house, and the company is NOT paid, it may file a
Feedback / Explanation: A mechanic's lien gives security to persons or companies that perform labor or furnish material to improve real property and can be filed when the owner has not fully paid for the work. Lis pendens is a recorded notice of a suit that may affect title to real property. A default judgment is granted when a creditor files for unpaid bills and wins the right to place a general lien on all the debtor's property to collect payment. A deficiency judgment is a personal judgment a lender may file against a borrower for the unpaid balance on a mortgage loan when a foreclosure sale has not produced enough cash to pay the loan balance.

Question: An owner conveys a life estate to her grandson and stipulates that on her death the estate will pass to her son-in-law. The son-in-law has a(n)
Feedback / Explanation: The owner's death will end the life estate conveyed to her grandson, which will then mean the holder of the remainder interest, the son-in-law, will have fee simple absolute ownership of the property. An estate for years is a leasehold estate that continues for any definite period of time. A legal life estate is a freehold estate created out of a provision.

Question: Because a homeowner failed to pay her real estate property taxes on time, the taxing authority imposed a claim against her property. This claim is known as a(n)
Feedback / Explanation: The claim is a tax lien. Unpaid real estate property taxes are a lien on the property as they are a charge against the property that provides security for a debt or an obligation for payment by the property owner. A deed restriction creates a limitation on the use of a property. An easement is the right of one party to use the real property of another for a specific use. A reversionary interest is the ownership interest belonging to the creator of a life estate when the ownership reverts to the original owner upon the end of the life estate.

Question: Four friends decide to purchase an investment property as co-owners and take title as joint tenants. All of the following are true EXCEPT:
A: all four will enjoy equal rights of possession of the property.
B: all four will have equal interests in the property.
C: all four will acquire their interests at the same time.
D: the four friends will each receive a separate deed for their share.
Feedback / Explanation: All concurrent ownership is created by one deed, which will establish either joint tenancy or tenants in common. Additionally, joint tenancy requires that four unities must be present: possession, interest, title, and time (PITT).

Question: A mechanic's lien would be properly classified as a(n)
Feedback / Explanation: A mechanic's lien is a specific lien as it affects a specific property and only that particular property. It is an involuntary lien placed on a property without the owner's consent. A general lien is the right of a creditor to have all of an owner's property, real and personal, sold to satisfy a debt. An equitable lien arises out of a written contract that shows the intention of the parties to charge a particular property as a security for a debt or obligation.

Question: Which of the following liens usually would be given highest priority in disbursing funds from a foreclosure sale?
A: A judgment rendered the day before foreclosure
B: A mechanic's lien for work started before the mortgage was made
Feedback / Explanation: Unpaid real estate property taxes have first priority and "move to the head of the line" when property is liquidated at a foreclosure sale. They are a statutory lien with priority over liens created by contract, such as mortgages. Unpaid real estate property taxes take priority over mechanic's liens and judgments, no matter when those liens were recorded.

Question: The owner has a large parcel of land surveyed into lots and streets and files a subdivision plat. Each lot can be legally described by use of which of the following?
Feedback / Explanation: The lot and block system is often used to describe property in subdivisions. The rectangular government survey system divides land into rectangles and describes those rectangles with principal meridians and base lines. The metes-and-bounds system outlines the perimeter of a parcel by starting at one point and ending at the same point. A street address is not a legal property description.

Question: Neighbor A uses Neighbor B's driveway to reach A's garage, which is on their property. B's attorney explains that ownership rights of A's real estate includes an easement appurtenant giving them the right to use B's driveway. In this case, B's property is the
Feedback / Explanation: In an appurtenant easement the parcel over which the easement runs is known as the servient tenement, and the neighboring parcel that benefits from the easement is known as the dominant tenement. B's property is the servient tenement, and A's is the dominant tenement. The easement does not create a leasehold estate. A license is a personal privilege with permission from a landowner to enter the land for a specific purpose.

Question: Which of the following terms refers to sole ownership by one person?
Feedback / Explanation: Severalty ownership is ownership by one person severed and cut-off from all others. Tenancy in common and tenancy by the entirety are forms of joint ownership. Community property is personal or real property belonging to spouses according to state laws.

Question: A section in the rectangular survey system contains
Feedback / Explanation: The answer is 640 acres per section. Each township contains 36 sections, and each section is 1 square mile, or 640 acres per section.

Question: A seller lists her property with a local broker, and the listing agreement excludes the seller's prized Iris flowers planted in the garden. The broker lists the exclusion in the MLS and notes it on the property brochure. But when a buyer makes an offer on the property, the plants are not mentioned anywhere in the contract. The seller accepts the offer. At closing, who has possession of the Iris plants?
A: The buyer, since they are personal property that transfers with the title
B: The seller, since personal property must be included to convey
C: The seller, since they were excluded in the listing and MLS
D: The buyer, since they are improvements to the land
Feedback / Explanation: Landscaping is considered to be an improvement and, once attached, is an appurtenance which transfers with the land via the deed. In this question, the seller excludes the Iris plants on the listing which implies they are planted, or otherwise appurtenant to the property. Since the seller forgot to exclude the plants in the purchase contract, which is what determines the legal status of appurtenant items, the plants would likely be considered appurtenant and would transfer with the property to the buyer.

Question: A tenant's lease has expired, but the tenant has not vacated the premises or negotiated a renewal lease. The landlord has not given the tenant permission to remain in the building. This situation is an example of:
C: an estate from period to period.
Feedback / Explanation: Tenancy (estate) at sufferance occurs when a tenant who lawfully possessed real property continues in possession of the premises without the landlord's consent after the lease rights expire, typically from an estate for years. An estate from period to period automatically renews unless notice is given. A tenancy at will permits a tenant to possess property with the landlord's consent for an unspecified term. An estate for years is a leasehold estate that continues for a definite period, whether that period is days, weeks, months, or years and has a definite expiration date.

Question: The principal difference between an estate for years and an estate from period to period is that an
A: estate for years is a life estate.
B: estate from period to period has no expiration date.
C: estate for years cannot be terminated.
D: estate from period to period must be in writing.
Feedback / Explanation: An estate from period to period, or periodic tenancy, does not have a specific expiration date, as it automatically renews until notice is given by the tenant or landlord. An estate (tenancy) for years has specific beginning and ending dates. Neither estate is a life estate. Each can be terminated. Both an estate for years and an estate from period to period must be in writing to be enforceable.

Question: Three friends were concurrent owners of a parcel of real estate. One of the friends died, and his interest passed according to his will to become part of his estate. The deceased friend was a
Feedback / Explanation: In a tenancy in common, the property of a deceased owner may pass to the heirs according to the will. If the deceased owner had been a joint tenant, the property would not have passed according to the will because in a joint tenancy the property interests would have transferred directly to the other two friends (co-tenants). The deceased owner was not a tenant by the entirety, a category reserved for married couples. Nor was the deceased owner a severalty owner because that form of ownership requires property to be held by only one person.

Question: A general contractor recently filed suit against a homeowner for nonpayment. The contractor now learns that the homeowner has listed the property for sale with a real estate broker. In this situation, which of the following will the contractor's attorney use to protect the contractor's interest?
Feedback / Explanation: A recorded lis pendens (a notice of action pending) warns any potential purchaser that a property is subject to a pending legal action. This notice would prompt a buyer to consider very carefully whether to proceed, but it would not absolutely prevent it. The contractor may also have the right to use a mechanic's lien to obtain payment. The lis pendens would give notice until the mechanic's lien was in place. An assessment is a special levy for a definite purpose, such as adding curbs or sewers in a neighborhood. A mortgage lien is a voluntary specific lien the borrower signs.

Question: To create joint tenancy in the ownership of real estate, there must be unities of
D: grantees, ownership, claim of right, and possession
Feedback / Explanation: The equal interests of possession, interest, and title must all occur at the same time for joint tenancy to be created. REMEMBER: PITT for the unities of title in joint tenancy.

Question: Which of the following is TRUE of condominium ownership?
A: The association pays the unit income taxes
B: The common elements cannot be sold separately
C: The association pays the unit real estate taxes
D: The ownership of a unit cannot be willed
Feedback / Explanation: Although an individual unit owner owns an interest in the common elements as a tenant in common, they may not be sold separately by a unit owner; unit owners do not have the same right to partition that other tenants in common have. Individual owners pay the real estate taxes for their units. Condominium owners may will or mortgage their fee simple ownership in a condominium.

Question: A purchaser of real estate learns that his ownership rights could continue forever and that no other person claims to be the owner or has any ownership control over the property. This person owns a
Feedback / Explanation: Fee simple is an absolute ownership, an inheritable interest "with no strings attached." A life estate is a non-inheritable freehold estate that ends with the death of the owner or the death of some other designated person. A remainder interest is tied to a life estate. A qualified fee estate exists as long as a determined use of the property continues.

Question: A tenant who holds possession of a landlord's property without a definite lease term but with the consent of the landlord has a
Feedback / Explanation: An estate (tenancy) at will gives the tenant the right to possess property with the landlord's consent for an unspecified term. It continues until it is terminated by either party giving notice. A tenancy at sufferance arises when a tenant continues to possess the property without the landlord's consent after the tenant's rights expire. Tenancy in common is a form of co-ownership in which each owner holds an undivided interest in a property as if each were the sole owner. A holdover tenancy is created when a tenant with an estate for years holds on to a property after the lease term expires when no new lease has been signed.

Question: A parcel of property was purchased by two friends. The deed they received from the seller at closing conveyed the property by name to each of the two friends without further explanation. The friends took title as which of the following?
Feedback / Explanation: The answer is tenants in common. When joint tenancy is not clearly stated in the deed, grantees take title as tenants in common to avoid the results of accidental joint tenancy. Friends are unable to hold community property or be tenants by the entirety as those forms of ownership are available only for married couples.

Question: A title search for a property under contract has revealed several outstanding liens against the property. Which of the following liens has highest priority?
A: A special assessment for the current year
B: A property tax lien for the current year
C: A judgment lien rendered and recorded last month
D: An outstanding first mortgage lien dated and recorded one year ago
Feedback / Explanation: Tax liens have priority over previously recorded liens and over a special assessment, which will also take priority over previously recorded liens. The priority of the other liens will be based on the dates they were recorded. Liens recorded first will have a first right to payment or priority.

Question: When defining ownership estates, the term estate refers to:
A: a person's legal rights in the land or property.
C: only the possessionary rights of tenants and owners.
D: the quantity of land as shown on a plat map for a property.
Feedback / Explanation: With respect to property ownership, the word estate refers to a person's legal use or the bundle of rights in land which, depending upon the type of estate, include the right to possess, transfer, encumber, et cetera. Estates are not tied to the physical quantity or the value of the land itself. A fee simple estate is the highest estate one can hold in land since it has the most or maximum rights to the land.

Question: The type of real estate ownership that is MOST all-inclusive is a
Feedback / Explanation: A fee simple absolute estate is the highest interest in real estate recognized by law. Fee simple ownership is absolute ownership in which the holder is entitled to all rights to the property. A life estate is a freehold estate that lasts as long as the life of the life tenant. A qualified fee estate is limited by specific conditions created by agreement or by law. A reversionary interest is that interest in property that exists when ownership of a life estate reverts back to the original owner at the end of the life estate.

Question: Under which type of lien are both the real property and the personal property of the debtor sold to pay the debt?
Feedback / Explanation: A judgment is a general, involuntary, equitable lien on both real and personal property owned by the debtor. A mechanic's lien is a specific lien against real property filed by a contractor, subcontractor, or supplier when an owner or contractor has not paid for work or supplies for improvements on a property. A real estate tax lien is a statutory lien against real property for nonpayment of taxes. An assessment lien is a special assessment levied on real property to fund public improvements to property.

Question: A married couple owns a farm together, with the right of survivorship. Their ownership is MOST likely
Feedback / Explanation: A married couple, in certain states, may use a special form of co-ownership called tenancy by the entirety. Each spouse has an equal, undivided interest in the property, with the right of survivorship. Severalty ownership occurs when real estate is owned by one person only. Tenancy in common and community property forms of ownership do not include the right of survivorship (in most states).

Question: An owner purchased an interest in a house in a resort beach community. The owner is entitled to the right of possession only between July 10 and August 4 of each year. Which type of ownership MOST likely has been purchased?
Feedback / Explanation: The owner holds an interest in property for a stated time each year, sharing the property according to a specified time plan. The owner's interest is a time-share. The owner of a condominium or a cooperative has a right of possession for as long as the ownership is not transferred. An individual does not own property through a trust, as a trust holds title to the real estate.

Question: A person who has complete control of a parcel of real estate is said to own a
Feedback / Explanation: The highest interest in real estate recognized by law is the fee simple or fee simple absolute estate in which the holder is entitled to all rights of the property. In a fee simple estate, the property may pass to the owner's heirs upon the death of its owner. A tenant's right to possess a property for the term of a lease is called a leasehold estate. A life estate is an interest in real property that ends when the owner dies. A defeasible fee estate is a fee simple estate that can exist as long as a certain use continues or provided that the property is used for a specific purpose.

Question: A property owner conveys a life estate to her uncle and names her son as remainderman. On the death of the uncle, the son will have what type of estate?
Feedback / Explanation: The son holds a remainder interest in the property, and will have a fee simple absolute estate upon the death of the uncle, the life tenant.

Question: The difference between a freehold and leasehold estate is
B: the right of survivorship for the tenant.
C: ownership versus the right to purchase.
D: who is obligated to pay the property expenses.
Feedback / Explanation: Freehold estates are estates of ownership while in a leasehold estate the property owner gives the tenant possession of the property. Tenants may agree to pay all the property expenses or share them with the owner or the owner can pay them all. The right to purchase is not a requirement for leasehold and there is no right of survivorship.

Question: Which of the following would be classified as a general lien?
Feedback / Explanation: A judgment lien is not a lien just on specific assets but on the assets of the debtor in general. A judgment applies to both the personal and real property of the debtor. Property tax liens are specific liens that have priority over other liens. A mechanic's lien may be filed against real property by a contractor or supplier for nonpayment of work or supplies. A property tax lien may be filed by a government for the nonpayment of real estate property taxes.

Question: A property owner contracted to have a swimming pool installed... This lien was MOST likely a:
Feedback / Explanation: A mechanic's lien may be filed by a contractor when an owner refuses to pay... It is a specific, involuntary lien.

Question: When one property is owned in severalty, the:
A: property may be owned by more than one person.
B: property owner may sell, will, or lease...
D: property may be co-owned by spouses.
Feedback / Explanation: Property owned in severalty is owned by one person... Owner may be an individual or corporation.

Question: A landowner divided much of her land... What type of easement will be granted?
Feedback / Explanation: Easement by necessity arises when there is no access without crossing anothers land.

Question: Legal descriptions are used in purchase contracts... EXCEPT:
B: property address with full zip code
Feedback / Explanation: Addresses can change and are not legal descriptions. Lots, metes/bounds, and govt survey are valid.

Question: Property owned by three cousins as tenants in common. One dies intestate (with heirs). Interest passes to:
B: The other two cousins in joint tenancy
Feedback / Explanation: Interest passes to deceased owner's heirs under intestate succession. Not to other co-owners.

Question: The priority of liens refers to:
B: Order liens are paid if property sold
Feedback / Explanation: Priority of liens = order in which claims are paid. General rule: first to record, first in right.

Question: Which of the following is a lien on real estate?
Feedback / Explanation: A recorded mortgage is a voluntary, specific lien. Easements/covenants/encroachments are encumbrances.

Question: Fixtures EXCEPT:
Feedback / Explanation: Fixtures determined by attachment, adaptation, agreement. A plug-in microwave is personal property.

Question: Normally, priority of general liens is determined by:
Feedback / Explanation: Priority rule = first recorded, first in right. Property taxes/special assessments always come first.

Question: Personal property includes EXCEPT:
Feedback / Explanation: Built-in bookcase = real property. Chairs, emblements, trade fixtures = personal property.

Question: Wooded area with trails between cotton mills and housing =
Feedback / Explanation: Buffer zones separate residential from nonresidential zones.

Question: 7-story building, zoning later changed to max 6 stories. TRUE?
Feedback / Explanation: Existing building becomes legal nonconforming use.

Question: Purpose of building code is to:
Feedback / Explanation: Building codes ensure structural safety.

Question: Sign restriction: max 3ft, owner wants 9ft revolving sign. To do legally:
Feedback / Explanation: Variance allows prohibited use.

Question: Govt powers EXCEPT:
Feedback / Explanation: Private subdivision controls = deed restrictions, not government powers.

Question: Zoning ordinances define land uses EXCEPT:
Feedback / Explanation: Rental = ownership type, not land use classification.

Question: To determine if location can be retail store, examine:
Feedback / Explanation: Zoning ordinances regulate permitted property uses.

Question: Developer must consider what when planning subdivision?
Feedback / Explanation: Master plan governs land use, utilities, housing, etc.

Question: Eminent domain requires:
Feedback / Explanation: Government must take for public use/purpose.

Question: Lien with highest foreclosure priority?
Feedback / Explanation: Property taxes/special assessments always first.

Question: Which tax targets homeowners in particular?
Feedback / Explanation: Real property tax is specific to homeowners.

Question: Buyers agent should confirm which doc in new subdivision?
Feedback / Explanation: Certificate of occupancy ensures new construction meets code.

Question: Grocery store in rezoned area (now residential) is:
Feedback / Explanation: Store existed prior, so its legal nonconforming use.

Question: Municipality establishes development goals in:
Feedback / Explanation: Master plan = comprehensive planning.

Question: Which is a property improvement?
Feedback / Explanation: Improvements = physical structures like coop, fence, well.

Question: "Bundle of rights" refers to rights:
Feedback / Explanation: Bundle of rights = possess, use, transfer, encumber, exclude.

Question: System of land ownership in U.S. =
Feedback / Explanation: Allodial = absolute ownership by individuals.

Question: Rights of owner along riverbank are:
Feedback / Explanation: Riparian rights = rivers/streams. Littoral = lakes/oceans.

Question: Deed says "subject to an easement." Burdened land is:
Feedback / Explanation: Subject to = encumbered (burdened).

Question: Mechanics lien is classified as:
Feedback / Explanation: Mechanics lien = statutory lien (created by law).

Question: A non-possessory interest in real property is also called:
Feedback / Explanation: A claim against, limitation on or liability against real estate is called an encumbrance. Encumbrances include liens, deed restrictions, easements, encroachments, and licenses. An encumbrance gives some other entity a non-possessory interest in a property and can restrict the owner's ability to transfer title to the property or lessen its value.

Question: A condominium community offers a swimming pool, tennis courts, and a biking trail. These facilities are MOST likely owned by the:
A: unit owners, in the form of proportional divided interests.
B: unit owners, in the form of percentage undivided interests.
C: corporation in which the unit owners hold stock.
Feedback / Explanation: A condominium is a living space independently sellable and regarded as real estate. Common elements like recreational facilities are owned by all the unit owners as a percentage of undivided interests.

Question: A construction lien is best described as:
Feedback / Explanation: A construction lien, also known as a mechanics lien, is a claim made against a property by a contractor or other professional who has supplied labor or materials. They are considered a specific lien.

Question: A deed conveys ownership to the grantee as long as the existing building is not torn down. What type of estate does this deed create?
A: Life estate pur autre vie, with the measuring life being that of the building
Feedback / Explanation: A determinable estate (defeasible) is created when a grantor transfers land conditionally. Upon the happening of the event or condition, the transfer may be void or subject to annulment.

Question: The ownership in a condominium is an estate in real property consisting of an undivided interest in common and a separate ownership of space. The estate in the defined space is:
A: A fee simple estate with a proprietary lease.
Feedback / Explanation: A fee simple (absolute) is the highest possible ownership interest in real property, and it applies to individual condo units.

Question: The maximum estate that a person may hold in land may be referred to as a(n):
Feedback / Explanation: Fee simple absolute is the highest form of land ownership possible.

Question: Which of the following individuals holds a license and is therefore not entitled to a claim of adverse possession?
A: a person who has been entering an orchard with permission, and taking apples every year for 30 years.
B: a person who has been in possession of the property for 19 years.
C: a person who has been parking his car on a neighbor's property for 20 years without permission.
D: a person who held property for five years after inheriting it from a parent, who was in adverse possession for ten years.
Feedback / Explanation: A license is a personal right that may be revoked at any time. Entering the orchard to pick apples, with permission, is a license.

Question: A man conveys a life estate to his sister. The property will pass to his niece upon his sisters death. Which BEST describes the nieces interest during the sisters lifetime?
Feedback / Explanation: The remainder is the future interest conveyed to the remainderman in the deed.

Question: When a company furnishes materials for construction and is not paid, it may file a(n):
Feedback / Explanation: A mechanic's lien is used by contractors/suppliers to recover unpaid labor or materials.

Question: An unmarried couple owns real estate. Man owns 1/3, woman owns 2/3. The form of ownership is:
Feedback / Explanation: A tenancy in common is a shared tenancy where each holder has a distinct, separately transferable interest.

Question: An ownership interest that permits multiple purchasers to buy interests in the same real estate is a:
Feedback / Explanation: A timeshare divides ownership or use rights into time periods.

Question: Zoning ordinances may regulate:
Feedback / Explanation: Zoning ordinances regulate use, lot size, placement, height, and density.

Question: A person with complete control over a parcel of real estate owns a:
Feedback / Explanation: Fee simple provides the most control over real estate, while other estates have conditions or time limits.

Question: Neighbors use your driveway via an easement appurtenant. Your property is the:
Feedback / Explanation: The servient tenement is the property burdened by the easement.

Question: An easement appurtenant is best described as:
A: right to use anothers property at any time for whatever reason.
B: may be terminated at any time by the holder.
D: belongs to the land and follows title.
Feedback / Explanation: An appurtenant easement runs with the land and applies regardless of ownership changes.

Question: An owner with a permanent right to cross over adjoining land has:
Feedback / Explanation: An easement gives a property owner rights over anothers property for a purpose.

Question: An easement is:
B: an encumbrance, but not a lien.
C: both a lien and an encumbrance.
Feedback / Explanation: Easements are encumbrances, but not liens.

Question: Permission to use property by giving an easement creates:
Feedback / Explanation: Easements are encumbrances that affect property rights.

Question: Condemnation of private property for public use is under:
Feedback / Explanation: Eminent domain allows governments to take private property for public good with compensation.

Question: Oscar, Andrew & Douglas are joint tenants. Douglas conveys to Joe. Afterward, Oscar & Andrew:
C: continue as joint tenants with Douglas.
Feedback / Explanation: Douglass conveyance breaks joint tenancy for his share. Joe becomes tenant in common. Oscar & Andrew remain joint tenants of 2/3.

Question: Personal property includes all EXCEPT:
Feedback / Explanation: Fixtures are attached and considered real property, not personal property.

Question: The process under eminent domain by which property is taken is:
Feedback / Explanation: Condemnation is the process governments use to exercise eminent domain.

Question: If dominant & servient tenements merge under one owner, what happens?
Feedback / Explanation: You cannot hold an easement over your own property; the easement ends.

Question: The state wants farmland for a highway. Does it have the right?
Feedback / Explanation: Eminent domain is the right; condemnation is the process.

Question: The right of eminent domain refers to:
A: orgs right to condemn property for community
B: governments right to acquire property for public use
D: every citizens right to own property
Feedback / Explanation: Eminent domain is the governments right to acquire property for public use.

Question: Stephen leased for six months and stayed without consent after. He now holds an:
Feedback / Explanation: Estate at sufferance = holding over after lease expiration without landlord consent.

Question: Which is personal property?
Feedback / Explanation: Harvested crops are movable and considered personal property.

Question: Susan lets Oscar store a camper in her yard temporarily without rent. This is a:
Feedback / Explanation: Permission for temporary use is a license.

Question: If a condo owner defaults on a mortgage, the remaining owners:
Feedback / Explanation: Default affects only the unit owner, not others.

Question: The sudden removal of land when a stream changes its channel is:
Feedback / Explanation: Avulsion = sudden change; accretion = gradual.

Question: Which of these is TRUE concerning a property held in joint tenancy?
A: A maximum of two people can own the real estate
B: There is always the right of survivorship
C: The fractional interests of the owners can be different
D: Additional owners may be added later
Feedback / Explanation: Joint Tenancy is a type of concurrent estate in which co-owners have a right of survivorship, meaning that if one owner dies, that owner's interest in the property will pass to the surviving owner or owners by operation of law.

Question: Under which of the following types of liens can both the real property and the personal property of the debtor be sold to pay the debt?
Feedback / Explanation: Most liens are against a specific property. A judgment lien, however, is a decision directed by the courts and can apply to whatever assets of the defendant's that it deems appropriate.

Question: Under the terms of a net lease, a commercial tenant usually would be responsible for paying:
Feedback / Explanation: A net lease requires the tenant to pay taxes, insurance, and maintenance costs in addition to rent. Common in commercial real estate.

Question: A partition suit is used for which of the following?
A: To allow construction of party walls
B: To force a division of property without all the owners consents
D: To change a tenancy by entireties to some other form of ownership
Feedback / Explanation: Partition suits are pursued when a co-owner wants to sell their share and others are opposed. Courts may order division or sale.

Question: Real property includes which of the following?
A: Appurtenances, rights that belong to the land, and all personal property.
B: Appurtenances, rights that belong to the land, and harvested crops.
C: Appurtenances, rights belonging to the land, and trade fixtures.
D: Appurtenances, rights that belong to the land, and attached personal property.
Feedback / Explanation: Real estate consists of land, buildings, natural resources, and appurtenances. Attached personal property (fixtures) becomes real property.

Question: Stephen leases store space to Rose for a restaurant, and Rose installs ovens, booths, counters, and equipment. When do these items become real property?
B: When the lease expires, if the items are not taken by the tenant
C: When Rose defaults on rental payments
Feedback / Explanation: Trade fixtures remain tenants property but if left behind at lease end, they become part of the real property.

Question: A tenants lease has expired, but the tenant has not vacated or renewed. The landlord has not given permission to stay. This is an example of:
A: an estate from year to year.
Feedback / Explanation: Tenancy at sufferance occurs when a tenant stays beyond lease expiration without landlord consent.

Question: The form of tenancy that expires on a specific date is a:
Feedback / Explanation: A tenancy for years is for a fixed period, with automatic termination on the end date.

Question: If four persons hold title as tenants in common, they:
A: would be unable to determine which portion of the land is theirs.
B: would have the right of survivorship.
C: must each own a one quarter interest.
D: could not transfer their interest without consent of the others.
Feedback / Explanation: Tenants in common share undivided interest in the entire property. They cannot claim specific physical portions.

Question: An owners bundle of rights is best described as:
A: Right to build to any height
B: Right of ownership including air rights, subjacent support, mineral rights
D: Right to build under old zoning laws
Feedback / Explanation: Bundle of rights includes possession, control, exclusion, enjoyment, disposition plus air rights, subsurface rights, and support.

Question: Which of the following is/are considered personal property?
Feedback / Explanation: Fixtures (furnace, tub, fireplace) stay with property. Patio furniture is movable, so its personal property.

Question: After a mechanic has filed a lien, he may enforce it by:
A: turning the debt over to collections.
D: filing a lawsuit within a specified time frame.
Feedback / Explanation: Mechanics liens must be enforced by lawsuit within state deadlines.

Question: Susans neighbors use her driveway under an easement appurtenant. Susans property is the:
Feedback / Explanation: The property burdened by an easement is the servient tenement.

Question: A portion of Joe's building was inadvertently built on Ginny's land. This is called an:
Feedback / Explanation: Encroachment = unauthorized intrusion of a structure onto anothers land.

Question: The word "improvement" refers to all of the following EXCEPT:
Feedback / Explanation: Trade fixtures are tenants removable items, not permanent improvements.

Question: Of the following, which could be a lease on real property?
Feedback / Explanation: Estate for years = leasehold for a fixed term.

Question: To create a joint tenancy, there must be unities of:
B: grantees, ownership, claim of right, possession.
Feedback / Explanation: Joint tenancy requires the four unities: possession, time, interest, and title.

Question: Seddons store was legal for 30 years, but new zoning prohibits food sales. The store is now:
Feedback / Explanation: Non-conforming use = grandfathered when zoning changes.

Question: Condo owner and apartment lessee differ, but legally both hold:
Feedback / Explanation: Both owners and lessees hold estates in real property (freehold vs. leasehold).

Question: Vivian erects a fence. Materials are converted to real estate by method of:
Feedback / Explanation: Fixtures are created through attachment (MARIA test: Method, Adaptability, Relationship, Intention, Agreement).

Question: Which of the following best describes the concept of "legal title" to real estate?"
A: Ownership of the bundle of rights to real estate
B: The right of a buyer or lender to obtain ownership under certain circumstances
D: Absolute proof of ownership of real estate
Feedback / Explanation: Owning title to real property commonly connotes owning the complete bundle of rights that attach to the property, including the right to possession. More accurately, someone who possesses all ownership interests owns legal title to the property. Legal title is distinct from equitable title, which is the interest or right to obtain legal title to a property in accordance with a sale or mortgage contract between the legal owner and a buyer or creditor. During the contractual period of time when ownership of legal title is contingent upon the contract, the buyer or lender owns equitable title to the property

Question: A person claims ownership of a parcel of real estate to a prospective buyer, stating that she has lived on the property for five years and nobody has ever bothered her. The claimant also shows the buyer a copy of the deed. The legal basis of this claim is referred to as
Feedback / Explanation: In proving real estate ownership, a person provides actual notice by producing direct evidence, such as by showing a valid will

Question: Constructive notice of ownership of a parcel of real estate is primarily demonstrated through
A: direct inspection to see who is in possession
Feedback / Explanation: The foremost method of imparting constructive notice is by recordation of ownership documents in public records, specifically, title records

Question: An owner transfers title to a property to a buyer in exchange for a consideration. This is an example of
Feedback / Explanation: Voluntary alienation is an unforced transfer of title by sale or gift from an owner to another party

Question: For a deed to convey title, it is necessary for the deed to be
Feedback / Explanation: Execution of a valid deed in itself does not convey title. It is necessary for the deed to be delivered to and accepted by the grantee for title to pass

Question: The only required clause in a deed of conveyance is one that
A: states restrictions and limitations to the estate being conveyed
B: states the parties and the type of estate being conveyed
C: states that the grantor has done nothing to impair title to the property being conveyed
D: states the grantor's intention, names the parties, describes the property, and indicates a consideration
Feedback / Explanation: Granting clause, or premises clause - the only required clause; contains the conveyance intentions; names the parties; describes the property; indicates nominal consideration

Question: The purpose of a covenant clause in a deed of conveyance is to
A: state the grantor's assurance or warrant to the grantee that a certain condition or fact concerning the property is true
B: state the grantee's promise to use the property in a prescribed manner
C: warrant that the grantor has never encumbered title
D: describe the consideration that the grantee promises to give in return for title
Feedback / Explanation: Covenant clauses present the grantor's assurances to the grantee

Question: The type of statutory deed that contains the most complete protection for the grantee is a
Feedback / Explanation: The general warranty deed, or warranty deed for short, is the most commonly used deed. It contains the fullest possible assurances of good title and protection for the grantee

Question: A person wishes to convey any and all interests in a property to another without making any assurances as to encumbrances, liens, or any other title defects on the property. This party would most likely use which of the following types of deed?
Feedback / Explanation: A quitclaim deed. A quitclaim deed transfers real and potential interests in a property, whether an interest is known to exist or not.  The grantor makes no claim to any interest in the property being conveyed and offers no warrants to protect the grantee

Question: A one-time tax levied on a property for purposes of recording a transaction is called
Feedback / Explanation: State law usually requires payment of a documentary stamp tax on a conveyance of real property

Question: If a person dies with no legal heirs or relations and has left no valid will, what happens to real property owned by that person?
A: It is taken by the state according to the process called escheat
B: It is reconveyed to the previous owner in the chain of title
C: It is taken by the title insurance company according to the process called involuntary alienation
D: It is conveyed to the highest bidder at a public auction
Feedback / Explanation: It is taken by the state according to the process called escheat, or reverts, to the state or county after all claims and debts have been validated and settled

Question: Just prior to passing away, a person tells two witnesses that she would like her estate to pass to her husband. One witness records the statement and signs his name. This is an example of
Feedback / Explanation: An unenforceable nuncupative will, made orally, and written down by a witness; generally not valid for the transfer of real property

Question: If a person having several heirs dies intestate, the property will
A: pass to heirs by the laws of descent and distribution
C: pass to the surviving spouse through elective share
D: pass to the surviving heirs according to the provisions of the will
Feedback / Explanation: If the decedent died without a valid will, the estate passes to lawful heirs according to the state's laws of descent and distribution, or succession

Question: A property owner can avert the danger of losing title by adverse possession by
A: recording proof of ownership in county title records
B: inspecting the property and evicting any trespassers found
D: filing a claim of right with the county recorder
Feedback / Explanation: An owner can avert the danger of involuntary alienation by adverse possession by periodically inspecting the property within statutory deadlines and evicting any trespassers found. The owner may also sue to quiet title, which would eliminate the threat of the adverse possessor's claim to legal title

Question: A hermit secretly lives in a cave on a 200-acre property. After twenty years, the person makes a claim of ownership to the property. This claim will likely be
B: upheld because of the length of possession
C: declined through the doctrine of prior appropriation
Feedback / Explanation: State laws may allow a real property owner to lose legal title to an adverse possessor. An adverse possessor is someone who enters, occupies, and uses another's property without the knowledge or consent of the owner, or with the knowledge of an owner who fails to take any action over a statutory period of time. To claim legal title, the adverse possessor must have notorious possession, which is possession without concealment

Question: The fundamental purpose of recording instruments that affect real property is to
C: give constructive notice of one's rights and interests in the property
D: assemble all relevant documents in a single place
Feedback / Explanation: State laws require the recording of all documents that affect rights and interests in real estate in the public real estate records of the county where the property is located. These public records, or title records, contain a history of every parcel of real estate in the county, including names of previous owners, liens, easements, and other encumbrances that have been recorded. Title records protect the public by giving all concerned parties constructive notice of the condition of a property's legal title: who owns the property, who maintains claims and encumbrances against the property

Question: What is "chain of title?"
A: The list of all parties who have ever owned real estate
B: The bundle of rights linked to the recorded title to a parcel
C: A chronology of successive owners of record of a parcel of real estate
D: Involuntary conveyance of title by statutory rules of descent
Feedback / Explanation: Chain of title refers to the succession of owners of record dating back to the original grant of title from the state to a private party. It is thus more than a mere list; it is a chronological list as reflected in title records

Question: The Torrens System differs from other title recording systems in that
A: title is conveyed only when conveyance is registered on the title certificate
B: the Torrens System requires prior approval of a transaction before it can be registered
C: the Torrens System is not recognized by title insurance companies
D: it allows unrecorded encumbrances to cloud title
Feedback / Explanation: The Torrens system differs from other title recording systems in that title passes only when the conveyance has been duly registered on the title certificate itself

Question: To be marketable, title must be
B: free of undisclosed defects and encumbrances
Feedback / Explanation: Marketable title is, by definition, one that is free and clear of undesirable and unknown encumbrances, claims, clouds, or other defects. Attorney's abstracts and title certificates do not guarantee that a title is marketable

Question: With the exception of a Torrens certificate, the best evidence of marketable title is
Feedback / Explanation: A title insurance policy is commonly accepted as the best evidence of marketable title. A title insurance policy indemnifies the policy holder against losses arising from defects in the insured title

Question: A property owner who possesses all ownership interest owns
Feedback / Explanation: Owning title to real property commonly connotes owning the complete bundle of rights that attach to the property, including the right to possession.  In turn, someone who possesses all ownership interests owns legal title to the property

Question: A mortgage loan transaction gives the lender the right to execute a strict foreclosure, which transfers legal title to the lender in the event of a default. With this contractual right, which type of title does the lender hold?
Feedback / Explanation: Equitable title is the interest or right to obtain legal title to a property in accordance with a sale or mortgage contract between the legal owner and a buyer or creditor. During the contractual period of time, when ownership of legal title is contingent upon the contract, the buyer or lender owns equitable title to the property.  With this contractual right, the lender has equitable title to the property

Question: The term "notice" is synonymous with
Feedback / Explanation: A person who has received actual notice has actual knowledge of something. Receiving actual notice means learning of something through direct experience or communication

Question: When the transfer of title to real estate uses a written instrument, the transfer is called
Feedback / Explanation: The use of a written instrument to transfer title to real estate is called a conveyance

Question: Which of the following is an example of a voluntary transfer of title to real estate?
Feedback / Explanation: Voluntary alienation is an unforced transfer of title by sale or gift from an owner to another party. A deed is the legal instrument used to effect a voluntary transfer of title to real estate

Question: Involuntary alienation occurs primarily by the process of
Feedback / Explanation: Involuntary alienation is a transfer of title to real property without the owner's consent. Involuntary alienation occurs primarily by the processes of descent and distribution, escheat, foreclosure, eminent domain, adverse possession, and estoppel

Question: A deed is a legal instrument used by an owner.  The owner in a conveyance is also known as the
Feedback / Explanation: A deed is a legal instrument used by an owner, the grantor, to transfer title to real estate voluntarily to another party, the grantee

Question: Which of the following statements applies if a grantee loses the deed?
A: The grantor can reclaim the property
B: There is no effect on the grantees title to the estate
C: Whoever finds the deed will automatically own the property
D: The grantee will lose the title to the estate by escheat
Feedback / Explanation: If the grantee loses the deed, there is no effect on the grantee's title to the estate. The grantor, for example, cannot reclaim the estate on the grounds that the grantee has lost the deed after it was delivered and accepted

Question: Which of the following statements is true regarding the effect of recording a deed?
A: Recording is necessary to make a deed valid
B: Recording is not necessary to make a deed valid
C: Recording the deed gives actual notice of the grantees ownership
D: Recording a deed is not in the grantee's best interest
Feedback / Explanation: Recording is not necessary to make a deed valid. However, it is in the grantee's best interest to do so. Recording the deed gives the public constructive notice of the grantee's ownership

Question: The habendum clause
A: recites restrictions and limitations to the estate being conveyed
B: assures the grantor owns the estate to be conveyed
D: describes the type of estate being conveyed
Feedback / Explanation: The habendum clause describes the type of estate being conveyed (fee simple, life, etc.)

Question: A deed which conveys to the grantee the promise from the grantor that I own and will defend against my acts only has received which of the following deeds?
Feedback / Explanation: In a special warranty deed, the grantor warrants only against title defects or encumbrances not noted on the deed that may have occurred during the grantor's period of ownership or trusteeship

Question: Which of the following statements is true about quitclaim deeds?
A: The quitclaim deed is typically used to clear title rather than convey it
B: The quitclaim deed covenant claims that the title will be defended in court
C: The quitclaim deed is the same as a special warranty deed
D: The quitclaim deed contains the warrant of seisin
Feedback / Explanation: The quitclaim is typically used to clear title rather than convey it. Where there is a possibility that prior errors in deeds or other recorded documents might cloud (encumber) the title, the relevant parties execute a quitclaim deed to convey "any and all" interest to the grantee

Question: A valid last will and testament for the transfer of real property will generally take which of the following forms?
Feedback / Explanation: A will can take on one of the following forms:  witnessed, holographic (in the testators handwriting, dated and signed), approved or nuncupative

Question: A single parent died intestate, leaving four surviving children. In this case, the estate passes to the lawful heirs according to the states laws of
Feedback / Explanation: If the decedent died without a valid will, the estate passes to lawful heirs according to the state's laws of descent and distribution, or succession

Question: To claim legal title, the adverse possessor must have
